adv_u01.text: Unit 1 - Present Continuous and Present Simple (1): Stative verbs
adv_u01.note: I think vs. I'm thinking - two meanings
adv_u01.exp:
Unit 1 at the Advanced level examines subtle cases when the same verb changes meaning depending on time.

Thinking verbs: think, consider, see, have, taste, smell in Continuous vs. Simple. I think he's right (opinion) vs. I'm thinking about leaving (process). I see what you mean (I understand) vs. I'm seeing a doctor (meeting). Features not available at lower levels.

adv_u02.text: Unit 2 – Present Continuous and Present Simple (2)
adv_u02.note: Temporary vs. permanent situations - advanced cases
adv_u02.exp:
Unit 2 deepens the distinction between Continuous (temporary/in process) and Simple (constant/actual).

Situations not obvious in B2: scientific facts (Water boils at 100°C), narrative in the present (So then she opens the door and sees...), commentary on an event in real time. Also: gradable vs. non-loadable use.

adv_u03.text: Unit 3 – Past Simple and Present Perfect
adv_u03.exp:
Unit 3 at the Advanced level analyzes contextual factors that determine the choice between Past Simple and Present Perfect.

The role of discourse context: “news” vs. "details". Differences between British and American versions. It's the first time I've been here vs. I went there in 2010. Perfect in different registers (formal vs. colloquial).

adv_u04.text: Unit 4 - Past Continuous and Past Simple
adv_u04.exp:
Unit 4 deepens the distinction between the past continuous and the past simple at level C1.

Past Continuous for the background setting in the narrative: It was raining, people were rushing home, when suddenly... Incompleteness: I was writing the report ≠ I wrote the report. while/when pairs. Diplomatic or softened form: I was wondering if you could help.

adv_u05.text: Unit 5 - Past Perfect and Past Simple
adv_u05.exp:
Unit 5 examines the Past Perfect as a tool for structuring time sequence in complex texts.

The Past Perfect is required: only when the order of events is not obvious without it. Temporary conjunctions: after, before, when, by the time, once, as soon as + Past Perfect. Narrative use: He had never seen such beauty before that day.

adv_u06.text: Unit 6 – Present Perfect Continuous and Present Perfect
adv_u06.note: Emphasis on process vs. on the result
adv_u06.exp:
Unit 6 analyzes the choice between Perfect and Perfect Continuous at an advanced level.

Key factors: action verbs vs. conditions; emphasis on duration (I've been working) vs. result (I've finished). Cases when both are possible: live, work, study, teach. Pragmatics: I've been trying to call you sounds with an apology/explanation.

adv_u07.text: Unit 7 - Past Perfect Continuous, Past Perfect and Past Continuous
adv_u07.note: Three past tenses in one narrative
adv_u07.exp:
Unit 7 teaches you to manage three past tenses simultaneously in a connected text.

Past Continuous - background. Past Perfect - previous event. Past Perfect Continuous - lasting previous action. She was exhausted. She had been working all night and had barely slept when the meeting started.Three levels of the past in one paragraph.

adv_u08.text: Unit 8 - Present and Past time: overview
adv_u08.note: Systematic review of the present and past
adv_u08.exp:
Unit 8 provides a systematic overview of present and past tenses in terms of function and context.

Four key questions: 1) when does it happen? 2) completed or not? 3) is the result or the process important? 4) is there a connection with the present? Exercises in choosing the right time in complex contexts. Summary table with pragmatic nuances.

adv_u09.text: Unit 9 – Will and be going to
adv_u09.note: Subtleties of choosing between will and be going to
adv_u09.exp:
Unit 9 at the Advanced level analyzes in detail the nuances of choosing between will and be going to.

Not only “spontaneously vs. planned": will for predictions from experience/knowledge; going to for visible signs. Style and register: willin formal/written. Both forms for predictions depend on the evidence. Emotional coloring.

adv_u10.text: Unit 10 – Present Simple and Continuous for the future
adv_u10.exp:
Unit 10 examines the use of present tenses for the future at level C1.

Present Simple for the future - only schedules and programs beyond our control: The conference starts on Monday. Continuous for personal plans with confirmation: I'm meeting her at 3. Subtle difference in the degree of “fixedness” of the event.

adv_u100.note: Such was / So great was / Hardly had I…
adv_u100.exp:
Unit 100 continues the theme of inversion: constructions with so/such, scarcely/hardly, no sooner.

So + adj + be: So cold was it that nobody went out. Such was + noun: Such was his anger that… Hardly/scarcely/no sooner + had + subject + V3…when/than: Hardly had she left when the phone rang.The highest degree of formality and expressiveness.

adv_u11.text: Unit 11 - Future Continuous and Future Perfect (Continuous)
adv_u11.note: I'll be working / I'll have finished / I'll have been working
adv_u11.exp:
Unit 11 examines rare future tenses in detail.

Future Continuous (will be + -ing): action in progress at a future moment, a polite question about plans (Will you be using your car tomorrow?). Future Perfect (will have + V3): completion by the moment. Future Perfect Continuous (will have been + -ing): duration to the moment.

adv_u12.note: The Prime Minister is to visit France.
adv_u12.exp:
Unit 12 is learning the construction be to + infinitive - a formal way of talking about plans and agreements.

Official plans and announcements: The President is to address the nation tonight. Instructions: You are to report to the manager. Condition: If this is to work, everyone must cooperate. Past: was to → was supposed to. Predominantly written, formal register.

adv_u13.text: Unit 13 - Other ways of expressing the future
adv_u13.note: be about to / be due to / be bound to
adv_u13.exp:
Unit 13 expands the repertoire of expressing the future beyond the basic forms.

Be about to + infinitive: is about to happen (The match is about to start). Be due to: on schedule (The train is due to arrive at 8). Be bound to: inevitably (She's bound to succeed). Be likely to: probably. Be on the point/verge of + -ing.

adv_u14.note: She said she would come. He was going to resign.
adv_u14.exp:
Unit 14 examines “the future from the point of view of the past,” a key concept for complex texts.

Would as the past will: I thought it would rain. Was going to: unrealized intention: She was going to call, but she forgot. Was to: a planned event. Was about to: almost happened. Important for indirect speech and narratives.

adv_u15.text: Unit 15 – Can, could, be able to, be allowed to
adv_u15.exp:
Unit 15 differentiates between ability (can/could/be able to) and permission (can/may/be allowed to) at level C1.

Could for past general ability vs. was able to for a specific achievement. Be allowed to is a more formal permission. Can't have + V3: impossibility in the past. Subtle pragmatic differences.

adv_u16.text: Unit 16 – Will, would and used to
adv_u16.exp:
Unit 16 parses will/would for typical behavior and used to for past habits.

Will for present characteristics: Oil will float on water. Annoyance: She will keep interrupting! Would = used to for past habits, but only actions (not states): We would go fishing. Narrative would in a literary text.

adv_u17.exp:
Unit 17 analyzes may and might as instruments of graded uncertainty.

Degrees of certainty: must (95%) > should (80%) > may (50%) > might (30%) > could (20%). Past: may/might + have + V3. Formal resolution: May I come in? Deontic vs. epistemic meaning. Register differences may vs. might.

adv_u18.note: Internal vs. external coercion on C1
adv_u18.exp:
Unit 18 at C1 level analyzes the subtle differences between must (subjective necessity) and have to (objective).

Register differences: must is more common in writing. Must for logical conclusions: It must be difficult. Past must: had to. Must in matters: persistence. Double meaning of some statements.

adv_u19.text: Unit 19 - Needn't, don't need to and don't have to
adv_u19.exp:
Unit 19 examines three nuanced ways to express the absence of need.

Needn't (modal): not necessary from the speaker's point of view. Don't need to: there is no need as a fact. Don't have to: lack of obligation. Needn't have + V3: performed an unnecessary action. Didn't need to + infinitive: there was no need (and maybe he didn't).

adv_u20.text: Unit 20 – Should, ought to and had better
adv_u20.exp:
Unit 20 compares three advice/recommendation constructs at C1 level.

Should/ought to: advice or weak commitment (interchangeable, but ought to is a little more objective). Had better: a specific situation with a hint of consequences. Should have/ought to have: reproach about the past. Shouldn't have: should not have been done (already done).

adv_u21.text: Unit 21 - Linking verbs: be, appear, seem, become, get
adv_u21.note: She seems tired. Didn't become angry. It got dark.
adv_u21.exp:
Unit 21 studies copular verbs that connect the subject to the nominal part of the predicate.

Basic connectives: be, seem, appear, look, feel, sound, smell, taste (+ adjective, not adverb). Dynamic links for state changes: become, get, grow, turn, go, come, fall. Examples:She fell ill. The leaves turned yellow. He went pale. Her dream came true.

adv_u22.note: Passive voice: functions and selections on the C1
adv_u22.exp:
Unit 22 analyzes the functions of the passive voice at the Advanced level: style, emphasis, information structure.

Three reasons to use the passive: the actor is unknown; the actor is obvious; emphasis on the object. Passive in scientific and official text. Choice between be + V3 and get + V3: get - more colloquial, often with a negative meaning: She got fired.

adv_u23.note: Being done / to be done / having been done
adv_u23.exp:
Unit 23 looks at passive forms of participles and infinitives.

Passive gerund: She hated being criticized. Passive infinitive: It needs to be done carefully. Perfect passive gerund: She regretted having been passed over for promotion. These forms are typical for complex sentences in formal texts.

adv_u24.text: Unit 24 - Different ways to use Passive
adv_u24.note: Passive with two objects and other advanced cases
adv_u24.exp:
Unit 24 looks at the passive in non-standard constructions.

Passive with two complements: She was given a prize / A prize was given to her. Impersonal passive constructions: It was decided that… Passive with get: colloquial version. Pseudo-passive (state): The door was open vs. was opened. Passive in different registers.

adv_u25.text: Unit 25 – Reporting with Passive: It is said that…
adv_u25.note: It is believed / said / reported / thought that…
adv_u25.exp:
Unit 25 examines passive constructions for neutral communication of information in academic and journalistic discourse.

Two options: It is said that she is brilliant = She is said to be brilliant. Verbs: believe, consider, expect, find, know, report, say, think, understand, claim, suppose. Past: She is said to have been the best.

adv_u26.text: Unit 26 – Wh-questions: who, whom, which, how, whose
adv_u26.note: Complex questions with prepositions and who/whom choices
adv_u26.exp:
Unit 26 deals with complex interrogative constructions at level C1.

Whom - formal object form of who: To whom should I address the letter? Preposition + which/whom in formal style. Whose in questions of belonging. Which vs. what when choosing. How + adjective/adverb for degree questions.

adv_u27.text: Unit 27 - Negative questions; echo questions; questions with chat-clauses
adv_u27.note: Isn't she lovely? She's leaving, is she?
adv_u27.exp:
Unit 27 analyzes non-standard question types: negative, echo questions and declarative questions.

Negative questions: rhetoric or expression of surprise/hope: Isn't she wonderful! Echo questions: rising question: She's from Japan? (really?) Declarative questions: You're leaving tomorrow, are you?

adv_u28.text: Unit 28 - Different types of objects after verbs
adv_u28.exp:
Unit 28 systematizes object structures after verbs at level C1.

Constructions: verb + object + noun (They elected him president); verb + object + adjective (He found the work difficult); verb + object + participle (I saw her leaving); verb + object + infinitive (She asked him to help). Analysis of the semantics of these constructions.

adv_u29.note: She gave me a book. / She gave a book to me.
adv_u29.exp:
Unit 29 analyzes ditransitive verbs in detail at an advanced level.

Two orders: verb + indirect object + direct object / verb + direct object + to/for + indirect object. Limitations: You can't always change the order. Verbs with to only: explain, describe, introduce, suggest. Pragmatics: information structure and theme/rheme.

adv_u30.note: Remember / understand / decide: what form and why
adv_u30.exp:
Unit 30 systematically parses verbs followed by a gerund or infinitive, with an emphasis on semantics.

Infinitive-only verbs: want, decide, hope, seem, tend. Only with gerund: enjoy, avoid, finish, risk. Verbs with both forms: difference in meaning (try, stop, remember, forget, regret, mean). A systematic approach to memorization through semantic groups.

adv_u31.note: begin doing/to do: cases without difference and with difference
adv_u31.exp:
Unit 31 continues the theme: verbs where the choice of form is determined by structure or meaning.

Verbs of perception (see, hear, watch, feel) + object + infinitive (full event) vs. -ing (in progress). Need/want + -ing = passive meaning: The car needs servicing. After adjectives: it's worth doing, it's no use trying, there's no point complaining.

adv_u32.exp:
Unit 32 systematizes the entire system of indirect speech at level C1.

Compulsory vs. optional time shift: if the information is current, the time does not shift: She said she is pregnant (still relevant). Full table of changes: must→had to, can→could. Changing deictic expressions: here→there, now→then, tomorrow→the next day.

adv_u33.note: Questions, commands, exclamations in indirect speech
adv_u33.exp:
Unit 33 examines indirect speech for questions, commands and emotional statements.

Indirect questions: direct word order, without ? : She asked where I had been. Yes/no questions: He asked if/whether I was ready. Commands: She told me not to interrupt. Exclamations: She exclaimed that it was incredible.

adv_u34.note: I wonder what she meant. The question is who…
adv_u34.exp:
Unit 34 studies wh-clauses as nominal, subject and additional clauses.

Wh-clause as an object: I know what you mean. As a subject: What she said surprised everyone. After nouns: The question of who will lead the team. In formal text: It is unclear whether…

adv_u35.text: Unit 35 – Reporting and alternation of tenses
adv_u35.note: When to shift time and when not to
adv_u35.exp:
Unit 35 analyzes when tense shifts in indirect speech are required, optional, or impossible.

No shift: general truths, current facts, short periods of time. With a shift: information is outdated or distancing is important. The difference in meaning: He said he works there (still works) vs. He said he worked there (neutral).

adv_u36.note: She suggested going / She proposed that we go
adv_u36.exp:
Unit 36 explores structures for paraphrasing sentences, orders and intentions.

Sentences: suggest + -ing / suggest + that + clause. Intentions: She said she intended to leave. Orders: verbs with infinitive: He ordered them to stop. She encouraged me to reconsider.Retelling verbs: advise, warn, encourage, beg, command, forbid.

adv_u37.note: She said she could / would / might / should
adv_u37.exp:
Unit 37 examines the features of indirect speech with modal verbs.

Some modals do not change: would, could, should, might, ought to, used to. Will → would, can → could, may → might, must → had to/must. The context determines: must can remain if the meaning is preserved. Transformation exercises with modals.

adv_u38.text: Unit 38 – Reporting with nouns and adjectives
adv_u38.note: His claim that… / Her refusal to… / the fact that…
adv_u38.exp:
Unit 38 shows that other people's words can be conveyed through nouns and adjectives, not just verbs.

Attitude nouns: claim, belief, suggestion, promise, refusal, denial, acknowledgement + that/to. Adjectives:He was reluctant to agree. She was adamant that...A more formal and compact way of reporting.

adv_u39.text: Unit 39 – Should in that-clauses; present subjunctive
adv_u39.note: It's important that he should come / that he come
adv_u39.exp:
Unit 39 examines two constructions for expressing requirements and recommendations in formal language.

British: should in that-clause: It's essential that everyone should be informed. American: subjunctive without change: It's essential that everyone be informed. Verbs: recommend, suggest, insist, demand, require, propose.

adv_u40.text: Unit 40 – Agreement between subject and verb (1)
adv_u40.note: Problems of subject and predicate agreement
adv_u40.exp:
Unit 40 deals with difficult cases of subject and predicate agreement.

Collective nouns: the team is/are playing (British: are). Subject with and: Tom and Jerry are. Neither...nor/either...or: agreement according to the nearest: Neither the students nor the teacher was there. Subject = infinitive or clause: singular.

adv_u41.text: Unit 41 – Agreement between subject and verb (2)
adv_u41.exp:
Unit 41 continues the theme of coordination in more complex cases.

Nouns in -ics: mathematics is, politics is (usually singular). The news is. A number of + plural vs. the number of + singular Dividing subject: one of those who is/are. Indefinite pronouns: everyone is, nobody knows.

adv_u42.text: Unit 42 – Agreement between subject and verb (3)
adv_u42.note: Agreement with relative clauses and complex subjects
adv_u42.exp:
Unit 42 completes the topic of agreement: complex subjects with clauses and numerical expressions.

Who/which/that: verb agrees with the antecedent: She is one of the students who are late. Numerals: Three million pounds was donated. More than one: singular. The majority of: plural. or units depending on the noun.

adv_u43.text: Unit 43 – Compound nouns and noun phrases
adv_u43.note: Car park / a two-hour delay / state-of-the-art
adv_u43.exp:
Unit 43 parses compound nouns and noun phrases in formal texts.

Compound nouns: car park, bookshop, science fiction (space/hyphen/continuous - no general rule). Pre-modification: a ten-minute walk, a state-of-the-art facility. Post-modification: a walk of ten minutes. Nominal groups in scientific and business texts.

adv_u44.note: a friend of mine / one person I know
adv_u44.exp:
Unit 44 distinguishes between a/an (article) and one (numeral/pronoun) in contexts where they are easily confused.

One for emphasis on number: I've told you one hundred times! one + noun for singular: She is one person I really admire. A is not replaced by one in article functions. Colloquial: one day, one morning (once).

adv_u45.text: Unit 45 - A/an, the and zero article (1)
adv_u45.note: Basic principles for choosing an article on C1
adv_u45.exp:
Unit 45 systematizes article choice through the concepts of definiteness, specificity and general reference.

Specific vs. general: The tiger is endangered (species) vs. A tiger is a dangerous animal (type). Zero article for general statements: Tigers are endangered. Thematic structure: a introduces, the continues. Unique Items: the sun.

adv_u46.text: Unit 46 - A/an, the and zero article (2)
adv_u46.exp:
Unit 46 parses articles for abstract nouns and generalizations.

Without an article: Love is blind. Science is fascinating. With the: specification: The love of money is the root of all evil. With a/an: type or instance: She showed a love of language. Set expressions with a zero article: at war, in love, by chance, on fire.

adv_u47.text: Unit 47 - A/an, the and zero article (3)
adv_u47.exp:
Unit 47 organizes articles into specific thematic groups.

Institutions by function vs. by place: in hospital (as a patient) vs. in the hospital (just there). Transport: by train (no article). Diseases: cancer (zero) vs. a cold, a headache (undefined). Musical instruments: play the piano.

adv_u48.exp:
Unit 48 deepens some and any to level C1: referential, non-referential and polar contexts.

Some - defined but unidentified: I read it in some book. Any in statements = no exceptions: Any student can apply. Some in sentence questions. Phraseological: some day, somehow, somewhere (uncertainty).

adv_u49.text: Unit 49 – No, none (of) and not any
adv_u49.note: No solution / none of them / not any reason
adv_u49.exp:
Unit 49 analyzes no, none and not any in terms of style and grammar.

No + noun: more emphatic and concise. Not any is a neutral alternative: There are no seats = There aren't any seats. None is an independent pronoun. None of + plural: singular verb. or plural (debatable). Stylistic preferences.

adv_u50.text: Unit 50 – Much (of), many (of), a lot of, lots (of)
adv_u50.exp:
Unit 50 differentiates quantitative expressions in terms of case and meaning.

Much/many - mainly in formal, negative and interrogative sentences. A lot of/lots of - informal statements. A great deal of is the formal equivalent of much. Many of + specific group: Many of the students.

adv_u51.note: Subtle differences, formal and colloquial contexts
adv_u51.exp:
Unit 51 analyzes four “all-encompassing” words at an advanced level.

All - the entire collection. Whole - integrity (without exceptions): the whole truth. Every - distributivity (each separately). Each - individualization. Postposition all/each: They all came / They each received a prize.

adv_u52.note: Fewer mistakes vs. less money (and the with fewer error)
adv_u52.exp:
Unit 52 parses few/little pairs and the fewer/less rule at level C1.

Fewer - for countables: fewer cars. Less - for uncountables: less traffic. Conversational blend: less people (common). A few/a little - positive connotation. The formal equivalent of little is: scarcely any, hardly any.

adv_u53.exp:
Unit 53 organizes relative pronouns at C1 level with an emphasis on formal variants.

Defining: who/that/which/whom/whose without commas. Descriptive (non-defining): only who/which/whose with commas. Formal version: whom (object). When that is impossible. Reference to the whole sentence: which.

adv_u54.text: Unit 54 – Whose, when, whereby and other relative words
adv_u54.note: The system whereby… / the reason why…
adv_u54.exp:
Unit 54 parses less frequent, but important for C1, relative words.

Whereby = by which: a system whereby students can apply online. Wherein: formal = in which. Whereupon: and after that. When: the moment when I saw her. Why: the reason why. The constructions are typical for legal, academic and business texts.

adv_u55.note: The company for which / the person to whom
adv_u55.exp:
Unit 55 breaks down prepositions in relative clauses: formal vs. informal position.

Conversational: the person I was talking to. Formal: the person to whom I was talking. A preposition is required at the end (informally) or before whom/which (formally). You can’t: the person to that / the person to who. Academic style requires a preposition before whom/which.

adv_u56.text: Unit 56 – Noun phrases in a sentence (Lesson 1)
adv_u56.exp:
Unit 56 analyzes noun phrases - a key element of complex texts.

Structure: determiner + pre-modifier + noun + post-modifier. Pre-modification: a beautiful old house. Post-modification: relative clause, prepositional phrase, infinitive phrase, participial phrase. Noun phrase density in academic texts.

adv_u57.exp:
Unit 57 goes into detail about noun phrase postmodifiers.

Postmodifiers: relative clause: the book that I wrote; prepositional phrase: the book on the shelf; infinitive: the first person to arrive; participle: the car parked outside; apposition: my friend Anna. Multiple modifiers in academic texts.

adv_u58.note: Leaving the room, she… / Not knowing what to do…
adv_u58.exp:
Unit 58 explores adverbial participial phrases (-ing and -ed) for condensed expression.

Active participial phrase (-ing): simultaneous or preceding action: Seeing the problem, she acted. Passive (-ed): Exhausted by the journey, he fell asleep. Perfect: Having studied hard, she passed.The subject of the phrase = the subject of the main clause.

adv_u59.exp:
Unit 59 continues the theme of adverbial participial phrases with complex examples.

Participial phrases after conjunctions: while working, when asked, if done correctly. Independent constructions (absolute constructions): The meeting over, everyone left. Formal style. Stylistic effects in literary texts.

adv_u60.exp:
Unit 60 parses reflexive pronouns in non-standard contexts in C1.

Amplifying: He built it himself. Idiomatic: make yourself comfortable, help yourself, enjoy yourself. After prepositions: She did it by herself. When the reflexive is NOT needed: She dressed quickly (not dressed herself). Each other vs. themselves: mutual vs. returnable.

adv_u61.note: One as a pronoun / general pronoun / one another
adv_u61.exp:
Unit 61 examines one/ones as a noun replacement and as a generic pronoun in formal language.

One (replacement): the red one, the ones on the shelf. General one (=people in general, formal): One should be careful. Colloquial equivalent: you. one another construction: reciprocity. Set expressions: one by one, one at a time.

adv_u62.note: I think so / I hope so / so they say
adv_u62.exp:
Unit 62 is exploring so as a substitute for a clause to avoid repetition.

I think so / I hope so / I believe so: so replaces a that clause. Denial: I don't think so / I think not (formally). Verbs: say, think, hope, suppose, expect, believe, imagine, be afraid. So for confirmation: So it seems / So they say.

adv_u63.note: She did so immediately. Such was his anger.
adv_u63.exp:
Unit 63 treats do so as a verb phrase substitute and such in formal constructions.

Do so: a more formal, precise verb phrase replacement: She was asked to leave and did so immediately. Do it: less formal. Such in inverted constructions: Such was the force of the explosion that... Book style.

adv_u64.note: She can swim and so can he / Ellipsis
adv_u64.exp:
Unit 64 explores ellipsis (omission) of auxiliary verbs to save speech.

After so/neither/nor: She can't drive, and neither can he. Opposite: Some people do and some don't. Instead of an infinitive: You can go if you want to (to instead of repetition). Text cohesion through ellipsis.

adv_u65.note: I'd like to come if I can.
adv_u65.exp:
Unit 65 examines the ellipsis of to-infinitive constructions in different contexts.

Short answer with to: I'd come if I wanted to. After want, plan, hope, need: I'd help but I can't afford to. In questions:Do you want to come? — I'd like to. You cannot omit to before the infinitive in all cases - rules of differentiation.

adv_u66.exp:
Unit 66 analyzes adjective positions at C1 level with attention to restrictions.

Attributive only (before a noun): a nuclear weapon (not the weapon is nuclear). Predicative only (after the linking verb): afraid, asleep, alive, alone, aware, glad. Change value: late/present/certain/right depending on position.

adv_u67.exp:
Unit 67 distinguishes between gradable and non-gradable adjectives and intensifiers used with each type.

Gradable (relative): cold, hot, big - you can say very big. Non-gradable (absolute): perfect, unique, dead, impossible - not very perfect, but absolutely perfect. Amplifiers: very/quitegradable; absolutely/completely/utterly with non-gradable ones.

adv_u68.exp:
Unit 68 continues the theme: the pragmatics of choosing amplifiers and collocation.

Absolutely brilliant/terrible (strong), fairly good/quite warm (moderate). Amplifiers-attenuators: a bit, slightly, rather, somewhat. Collocations: perfectly reasonable, completely wrong, highly unlikely. Register and expressiveness: conversational vs. writing.

adv_u69.text: Unit 69 – Participle adjectives and compound adjectives
adv_u69.exp:
Unit 69 looks at participial and compound adjectives.

Participial: -ing (active) vs. -ed (passive/state): a tiring journey / a tired traveler. Compounds: hyphen when used attributively: a well-known writer / She is well known. Productive models: air-conditioned, purpose-built, computer-generated.

adv_u70.note: Eager to learn / capable of doing / certain that
adv_u70.exp:
Unit 70 systematizes adjectives with complementary constructions.

+ to-infinitive: eager to, likely to, reluctant to, willing to, able to. + of + -ing: capable of, fond of, tired of. + that-clause: certain that, sure that, aware that, surprised that. Adjectives with a subject in the construction: kind of you to, easy for us to.

adv_u71.text: Unit 71 – Adjectives and adverbs: subtle differences
adv_u71.note: He spoke loudly. He is a loud speaker.
adv_u71.exp:
Unit 71 at level C1 examines non-standard cases of adjectives and adverbs.

Adjectives in narrative commentary: He smiled happily vs. He was a happy man. Clean as an adverb: I clean forgot. Semantic differences: highly/high, deeply/deep, widely/wide. Position of the adverb in various constructions. Reduced adjectives in speech.

adv_u72.note: Comparison on C1: ellipse and special constructions
adv_u72.exp:
Unit 72 explores complex aspects of degrees of comparison: double comparison, parallelism, ellipsis.

The more you study, the better you get. Double comparison: more and more difficult. Excellent with Present Perfect: the best I've ever seen. Ellipsis in comparatives: He drives faster than I thought (he would).

adv_u73.note: Than expected / as planned / as good as it gets
adv_u73.exp:
Unit 73 analyzes comparative phrases and clauses in complex sentences.

Incomplete comparatives: more than expected, better than planned. The same as: as good as it gets. No sooner…than: No sooner had I arrived than it started raining. Rather than as an alliance. Comparisons with the infinitive: She's too clever not to notice.

adv_u74.note: Position of adverb in a sentence: beginning, middle, end
adv_u74.exp:
Unit 74 organizes the positions of adverbs in a sentence in terms of style and meaning.

Start: Quickly, she left (emphasis). Middle (before the main, after the auxiliary): She quickly finished. End: Neutral position for course of action. Changing the position changes the focus: He only drinks wine vs. He drinks only wine.

adv_u75.exp:
Unit 75 examines the order of several circumstances at the end of a sentence.

Order: mode → place → time: She worked hard in the library every day. Cannot be broken: She worked every day in the library hard. Circumstances of the goal: a place in front of the goal. Several adverbs of image: from less to more specific.

adv_u76.text: Unit 76 – Adverbs of place, direction, regularity and time
adv_u76.exp:
Unit 76 classifies adverbs by type and examines the nuances of their position.

Places: here, there, somewhere, everywhere, nowhere. Directions: home, away, abroad, indoors. Time: soon, recently, already, still. Regularities: always, often, occasionally, rarely, never. Frequentative adverbs in academic texts.

adv_u77.text: Unit 77 – Degree adverbs and focus adverbs
adv_u77.note: very / extremely / even / just / only / almost
adv_u77.exp:
Unit 77 distinguishes between adverbs of degree (intensification/weakening) and focusing adverbs (narrowing of reference).

Degree: very, extremely, incredibly, fairly, quite, rather, somewhat. Focus (change the meaning of the sentence depending on the position): only, even, just, mainly, also, too, merely, simply. Only she knows vs. She only knows - different meanings.

adv_u78.text: Unit 78 – Comment adverbs and viewpoint adverbs
adv_u78.exp:
Unit 78 examines adverbs of comment and adverbs of point of view - important for academic and professional writing.

Comment adverbs: the speaker's assessment of the situation: unfortunately, surprisingly, incredibly, obviously, clearly, naturally. Viewpoint adverbs: point of view: personally, frankly, technically, scientifically. Position: usually at the beginning or end of a sentence, separated by a comma.

adv_u79.note: When / as soon as / once / until / by the time
adv_u79.exp:
Unit 79 systematizes subordinate tenses at level C1.

Conjunctions with different nuances: when (general); as soon as, the moment (that) (immediately after); once (after completion); until/till (until the moment); by the time (to the moment, with Perfect). Never Future in subordinate tenses: when she comes (not: will come).

adv_u80.text: Unit 80 - Giving reasons: as, because; for and with
adv_u80.note: As / because / since / for / with - all methods of cause
adv_u80.exp:
Unit 80 compares all means of expressing cause in English.

Because is the basic conjunction of cause (new information, answer to “why”). As/since - known/obvious reason: As it was raining, we stayed in. For - formal, old-fashioned. With: With all this noise, I can't concentrate.

adv_u81.text: Unit 81 – Purposes and results: in order to, so as
adv_u81.note: She left in order to avoid argument.
adv_u81.exp:
Unit 81 examines objective and result clauses at level C1.

Objective: to-infinitive / in order to / so as to. For different subjects: so that she could / so that + clause. Result: so + adj/adv + that: She spoke so quickly that nobody understood. Such + noun + that: It was such a shock that he fainted.

adv_u82.text: Unit 82 – Contrast clauses: although, while, while, whereas
adv_u82.note: Although / even though / while / whereas / despite
adv_u82.exp:
Unit 82 systematizes all means of expressing opposition/concession.

Although/though/even though - concession conjunctions. While/whilst/whereas - a contrast between two ideas: While he's good at maths, she's better at languages. Despite/in spite of + noun/-ing. However/nevertheless/nonetheless are adverbs of connection. Stylistic differences.

adv_u83.exp:
Unit 83 systematically examines real and potential conditions at level C1.

Type 0 (always true): If you heat iron, it expands. Type 1 (real): If you work hard, you'll succeed. Type 2 (unreal): If you worked hard, you'd succeed. Options in the main clause: might, could, should instead of would.

adv_u84.exp:
Unit 84 looks at Type 3 and mixed conditionals.

Type 3: If I had studied harder, I would have passed. Mixed (past + present): If I had taken that job, I would be living in London now. Mixed (present + past): If she were more careful, she wouldn't have made that mistake.

adv_u85.text: Unit 85 - If I were you; imagine he were...
adv_u85.exp:
Unit 85 parses the subjunctive were and formal inverted conditionals.

Were for all persons in non-real conditions: If I were you / Were I you. Inversion instead of if (formal): Were she to call, I'd answer. Had I known, I'd have helped. Should you need help, call me.Typical of business/legal language.

adv_u86.text: Unit 86 – If not and unless; if and whether
adv_u86.note: Unless it rains / I don't know whether
adv_u86.exp:
Unit 86 distinguishes between similar conditionals.

Unless = if not: with only a single condition. You cannot replace unless where the meaning is “even if not”: I'd help you if you asked (you cannot unless). If vs. whether: in indirect questions and alternatives: I don't know whether to go or stay.

adv_u87.text: Unit 87 - How to combine parts of a complex sentence
adv_u87.exp:
Unit 87 systematizes connecting words to organize complex texts.

Addition: moreover, furthermore, in addition, besides. Contrast: however, nevertheless, on the other hand, in contrast. Consequence: therefore, as a result, consequently, thus. Time: meanwhile, subsequently, previously. Key to Academic Writing.

adv_u88.text: Unit 88 – Prepositions of place and movement
adv_u88.note: Along / across / past / through - subtle differences
adv_u88.exp:
Unit 88 deals with prepositions of place and motion at C1 level, where subtle differences are important.

Along - along the line. Across - across or along a plane. Through - through three-dimensional space. Past - by. Towards - in a direction (not necessarily reaching). Against - opposite / contrary to. Around - around / approximately.

adv_u89.note: Between two / among many - and exceptions
adv_u89.exp:
Unit 89 distinguishes between between and among, subject to exceptions on C1.

Basic rule: between - two/well-defined; among - three or more/fuzzy group. Exceptions: between for clear several: choose between three options. Amongst - literary version. Idioms: between you and me, among other things.

adv_u90.note: At noon / by then / over the years / throughout
adv_u90.exp:
Unit 90 examines complex prepositions of time at level C1.

Over - for the period: over the years, over time. Throughout - throughout: throughout the year. Within - within: within a week. Towards - towards the end of the period: towards the end of the month. Prior to - before (the formal equivalent of before).

adv_u91.text: Unit 91 - The preposition except and its variants
adv_u91.note: Except / except for / apart from / but
adv_u91.exp:
Unit 91 studies prepositions of exception and their interchangeability.

Except (for): Everyone passed except (for) Tom. Apart from: 1) exception: Apart from Tom, everyone passed. 2) addition: Apart from being expensive, it's also slow. But in the meaning of “except” (with all, no, nobody, everything): nobody but me.

adv_u92.exp:
Unit 92 systematizes prepositions with verbs with an emphasis on C1-level.

Pairs with different prepositions: agree with (person) vs. agree to (offer) vs. agree on (something specific). Charge with (blame) vs. charge for (take payment). Aspectual differences: talk to vs. talk about vs. talk over.

adv_u93.note: Effect on / cause of / exception to / attitude towards
adv_u93.exp:
Unit 93 examines stable combinations of nouns with prepositions, important for written speech C1.

Effect on, reason for, cause of, lack of, exception to, towards/to, access to attitude, impact on, response to, solution to, increase in, change in. Key to Academic Writing. After the preposition there is a gerund: the reason for postponing.

adv_u94.exp:
Unit 94 systematizes phrasal verbs at level C1: three-component and inseparable.

Inseparable: look after, come across, put up with. Three-component: look forward to, come up with, get away with, make up for, run out of, put up with. After three-components there is a gerund or a noun. Phrasal register vs. single-word synonyms: put off = postpone.

adv_u95.text: Unit 95 - There is, there was, etc.
adv_u95.note: There + extended structures in academic text
adv_u95.exp:
Unit 95 deconstructs there is/are/was at level C1: information structure and style.

There is/are - brings new information into focus. Extended structures: There appears to be / There tends to be / There is believed to be. There is no need to / There is no point in. Idiomatic: There's no knowing / There's no telling.

adv_u96.text: Unit 96 - It as a formal subject (lesson 1)
adv_u96.note: It is clear that / It was decided that
adv_u96.exp:
Unit 96 systematizes constructions with formal it as a tool of objective style.

It is + adjective + that-clause: It is clear/obvious/important that... It is + V3 + that: It was decided that... Emphasis on the action, not the doer. These structures are the basis of academic, scientific and business writing. Analysis with the passive.

adv_u97.text: Unit 97 - It as a formal subject (lesson 2)
adv_u97.note: It seems / It appears / It turns out
adv_u97.exp:
Unit 97 continues the theme of formal it: semaphore verbs.

It seems/appears/turns out/emerges + that. It happens/so happens/chances + that. Strengthening: As it happens. It follows that / It means that / It implies that. Constructions for presenting conclusions and assumptions in formal texts.

adv_u98.note: Cleft sentences: What I need is… / It was Tom who…
adv_u98.exp:
Unit 98 examines cleft sentences for emphasis.

It + be + focus + that/who-clause: It was Maria who called. What + clause + be + focus: What I need is a holiday. Pseudo-cleft: What she did was (to) help everyone. A powerful rhetorical tool for highlighting a key point information.

adv_u99.note: Never have I seen / Only then did I realize
adv_u99.exp:
Unit 99 introduces subject-verb inversion as a rhetorical tool.

After negative adverbs at the beginning: Never have I seen, Rarely does she. After Only + adverbial: Only then did I understand. After Not only…but also: Not only did he arrive late, but he also forgot his keys. Characteristic of formal writing and rhetoric.

el_u01.text: Unit 1 – Am, is, are and Present Simple
el_u01.note: am / is / are · I work, she works
el_u01.exp:
Unit 1 combines the verb to be (am/is/are) and the basic form of the Present Simple.

The lesson explains how to choose the correct form of am/is/are depending on the subject, and shows the structure of affirmative sentences in the present simple tense. This is the foundation of all English grammar.

el_u02.note: Is she a teacher? Are they ready?
el_u02.exp:
Unit 2 teaches you to build questions with the verb to be.

In a question, am/is/are comes before the subject: She is → Is she?. General questions, short answers Yes, I am / No, she isn't, as well as special questions with the words where, what, who are covered.

el_u03.note: She is reading. They are working.
el_u03.exp:
Unit 3 introduces the Present Continuous - time for actions happening right now.

Formula: am/is/are + verb-ing. The lesson explains when to use this tense (at the moment, now), and examines the rules for writing the ending -ing (double consonant, omission -e).

el_u04.text: Unit 4 - Questions in the Present Continuous
el_u04.note: Are you working? What is she doing?
el_u04.exp:
Unit 4 teaches you how to construct questions and negatives in the Present Continuous.

Are you working? - Yes, I am. / No, I'm not.The lesson shows the word order in general and special questions: What are you doing? Where is he going?is a question word + am/is/are + subject + verb-ing.

el_u05.note: I work. She works. Do you work?
el_u05.exp:
Unit 5 goes into detail about the Present Simple - tense for constant facts, habits and schedules.

The lesson explains adding the ending -s/-es for the third person singular (he/she/it), exceptions (go → goes, do → does), and time markers: always, usually, often, sometimes, never.

el_u06.text: Unit 6 - Negations in the Present Simple (I don't)
el_u06.note: I don't like it. She doesn't work.
el_u06.exp:
Unit 6 teaches you how to form negatives in the Present Simple using the auxiliary verbs do/does.

Formula: don't + infinitive (I/you/we/they) and doesn't + infinitive (he/she/it). ⚠️ After doesn't the main verb always comes without ending -s: She doesn't work (not doesn't works).

el_u07.text: Unit 7 - Questions in the Present Simple
el_u07.note: Do you work? Does she like it?
el_u07.exp:
Unit 7 teaches you how to construct questions in the Present Simple with the auxiliary verbs do/does.

Word order: Do/Does + subject + infinitive. Special questions:Where do you live? What does she do?Short answers: Yes, I do / No, she doesn't.

el_u08.text: Unit 8 - Present Simple vs Present Continuous
el_u08.note: I work every day. I'm working now.
el_u08.exp:
Unit 8 compares two important tenses: Present Simple for regular actions and Present Continuous for actions in the moment.

Key markers: always/usually/never → Present Simple; now/at the moment/look! → Present Continuous. Particular attention is paid to stative verbs that are not used in the Continuous: know, like, want, need.

el_u09.text: Unit 9 – I have got or I have
el_u09.note: Have you got a car? / Do you have a car?
el_u09.exp:
Unit 9 explains the difference between have got (British) and have (American) in the meaning of possession.

Have got: I've got a car. She's got blue eyes. Formation of questions and negatives: Have you got…? / I haven't got... - without the auxiliary do, which distinguishes this construction from the usual have.

el_u10.text: Unit 10 – Was / were (past tense to be)
el_u10.note: I was tired. They were at home.
el_u10.exp:
Unit 10 explores the past tense of the verb to be: was (I/he/she/it) and were (you/we/they).

The lesson covers affirmative, negative sentences and questions: Was she at home? - Yes, she was. / No, she wasn't. Common mistakes: mixing was/were and using be instead.

el_u100.note: If I were rich, I would travel.
el_u100.exp:
Unit 100 examines the second type of conditional sentences for unrealistic/unlikely situations.

Structure: If + Past Simple, would + infinitive. If I had more money, I would buy a car (I don’t have money - unrealistic). With to be: If I were you, I would... (formally - were for all persons). Difference from First Conditional: lower probability.

el_u101.note: the man who called / the book that I read
el_u101.exp:
Unit 101 introduces relative clauses with who and that.

Who - for people: the man who called me. That/which - for items: the book that I borrowed. The relative clause clarifies what kind of person/object we are talking about. Place: immediately after the noun it refers to.

el_u102.note: the place where / the day when / the reason why
el_u102.exp:
Unit 102 expands on the topic: relative words where, when, why, whose.

Where - for places: the town where I grew up. When - for time: the day when we met. Whose - affiliation: the woman whose car was stolen. When the relative pronoun is an object, it can be omitted: the film (that) I saw.

el_u103.text: Unit 103 – Prepositions of time: at, on, in
el_u103.note: at 5pm / on Monday / in March / in 2020
el_u103.exp:
Unit 103 explains the choice of prepositions of time at, on, in.

At - exact time, holidays: at 8 o'clock, at Christmas. On - days of the week, dates: on Monday, on 5th May. In - months, years, seasons, parts of the day: in March, in 2020, in the morning. Exceptions: at night, at the weekend, in the afternoon.

el_u104.note: from 9 to 5 / until Friday / since Monday / for 3 hours
el_u104.exp:
Unit 104 looks at time period prepositions.

From … to/till: from 9 to 5. Until/till - until the moment: Wait until Friday. Since - from what moment (from the point until now), with Perfect: since Monday. For - duration: for three hours. ⚠️ Since is the starting point, for is the duration.

el_u105.note: before lunch / after work / during the film
el_u105.exp:
Unit 105 examines prepositions and conjunctions that connect events in time.

Before and after can be prepositions (after dinner) and conjunctions (before you leave). During - preposition (before a noun): during the film. While - conjunction (before the subordinate clause): while I was watching. ⚠️ During ≠ while - different parts of speech.

el_u106.text: Unit 106 – Prepositions of place: in, at, on (lesson 1)
el_u106.note: in the room / at the door / on the table
el_u106.exp:
Unit 106 explains the basic meanings of prepositions of place in, at, on.

In - inside: in the room, in the box, in Paris. On - on the surface: on the table, on the wall, on the floor. At - at a point/place: at the door, at the bus stop, at home. Also: in bed, on the left, at the top.

el_u107.text: Unit 107 – Prepositions of place: in, at, on (lesson 2)
el_u107.note: at work / in hospital / on holiday
el_u107.exp:
Unit 107 examines set expressions with prepositions of place in everyday situations.

At: at work, at school, at the station. In: in hospital, in prison, in the city centre. On: on holiday, on a trip, on the right/left. Some expressions need to be remembered as ready-made blocks: in the end, at the end, on the way.

el_u108.text: Unit 108 – Prepositions of place: to, in, at (lesson 3)
el_u108.note: go to school / arrive in London / arrive at the station
el_u108.exp:
Unit 108 examines prepositions of movement and arrival: to, in, at.

To - direction: go to work, travel to France. Arrive in - to a large populated area: arrive in London. Arrive at - to a specific location: arrive at the airport. Exceptions without to: go home, arrive home, come here.

el_u109.text: Unit 109 – Under, behind, opposite and others
el_u109.note: under the table / behind the door / opposite the bank
el_u109.exp:
Unit 109 examines prepositions that describe the relative position of objects in space.

Under - under. Above/over - above. Behind - behind. In front of - in front. Opposite - opposite. Between - between (two). Among - among (several). Next to / beside - next to.

el_u11.note: I worked. She went. They saw.
el_u11.exp:
Unit 11 introduces the Past Simple - the past simple tense for completed actions in the past.

Regular verbs add -ed: worked, played, stopped. Irregular verbs have special forms: go→went, see→saw, buy→bought. Markers: yesterday, last week, ago, in 2010.

el_u110.text: Unit 110 – Up, over, through and prepositions of direction
el_u110.note: go through the tunnel / walk up the stairs
el_u110.exp:
Unit 110 parses prepositions and adverbs of direction of movement.

Up (up), down (down), along (along), across (across), through (through, through), over (through/above), past (past), into/out of (in/out), onto/off (on/from the surface). Helps accurately describe the route of travel.

el_u111.text: Unit 111 - On, at, by, with, about
el_u111.note: by car / with a pen / about the problem / on TV
el_u111.exp:
Unit 111 systematizes prepositions with different meanings in set expressions.

By: means of transport (by car), passive agent (written by), method (by accident). With: tool (write with a pen), accompaniment. About: topic (talk about). On: on TV, on the radio, on holiday.

el_u112.note: good at / interested in / afraid of
el_u112.exp:
Unit 112 studies stable combinations of adjectives with prepositions.

Frequent combinations: good/bad at, interested in, afraid of, sorry for/about, different from, similar to, married to, proud of, tired of, bored with, responsible for. After the preposition there is a gerund: She's good at cooking. You need to memorize it together with the preposition.

el_u113.note: wait for / listen to / look at / look for
el_u113.exp:
Unit 113 examines stable combinations of verbs with prepositions (prepositional verbs).

The most common: wait for, listen to, look at, look for, talk about, think about, believe in, depend on, belong to, happen to, apply for. The question always has a preposition at the end: What are you waiting for? Who does this belong to?

el_u114.note: turn on/off, put on/take off, get up
el_u114.exp:
Unit 114 introduces the concept of phrasal verbs - verbs with postpositions that change meaning.

Examples: turn on/off (turn on/off), put on/take off (put on/take off), get up (get up), look up (look up in the dictionary), give up (throw). If there is a noun object, it can come between the verb and the postposition: turn the TV off / turn off the TV.

el_u115.note: break down, carry on, find out, look forward to
el_u115.exp:
Unit 115 continues the theme of phrasal verbs - more complex and frequently used combinations.

Break down - break down. Carry on - continue. Find out - find out. Look forward to - look forward to (after - gerund: looking forward to seeing you). Run out of - end. Set off - hit the road.

el_u12.text: Unit 12 - Past Simple: Question and Negation
el_u12.note: Did you work? I didn't go.
el_u12.exp:
Unit 12 teaches you to form questions and negatives in the Past Simple using the auxiliary did/didn't.

⚠️ After did/didn't the main verb is in the infinitive: Did she go? - Yes, she did. / She didn't go (not didn't go). Special questions:Where did you go? What did they do?

el_u13.note: I was reading when she called.
el_u13.exp:
Unit 13 studies the Past Continuous - an action that happened at a certain moment in the past.

Formula: was/were + verb-ing. Typical use: background action + interruption action: I was watching TV when the phone rang. Questions: What were you doing at 8 p.m.?

el_u14.text: Unit 14 - Past Simple vs Past Continuous
el_u14.note: She was cooking when he arrived.
el_u14.exp:
Unit 14 compares two past tenses: lasting action (Past Continuous) vs. interrupting/completed (Past Simple).

Conjunctions: when - moment of interruption; while - parallel ongoing actions. Example:While I was sleeping, she was studying. I fell asleep while I was reading.

el_u15.note: I have done. She has gone.
el_u15.exp:
Unit 15 introduces the Present Perfect - the connection between the past and the present.

Formula: have/has + past participle (V3). Used when the result of a past action in the present is important: I've lost my keys (and now I don't have the keys). Part of the irregular verbs: go→gone, see→seen, write→written.

el_u16.text: Unit 16 – Present Perfect: just, already, yet
el_u16.note: I've just eaten. Have you finished yet?
el_u16.exp:
Unit 16 parses the Present Perfect beacon keywords: just, already and yet.

Just - just (I've just arrived); already - already (earlier than expected, in the affirmative: She's already left); yet - yet/already (in questions and negations: Have you eaten yet? I haven't done it yet).

el_u17.text: Unit 17 – Present Perfect: Have you ever...?
el_u17.note: Have you ever been to Paris?
el_u17.exp:
Unit 17 teaches you to use the Present Perfect with the adverb ever to ask questions about life experiences.

Have you ever been to Japan? - “Have you ever been to Japan?” Negation with never: I've never tried sushi. The difference is also discussed: I've been to Paris (experience, returned) vs. I've gone to Paris (left and still there).

el_u18.note: I have been working for 3 hours.
el_u18.exp:
Unit 18 introduces the Present Perfect Continuous - a tense for actions that began in the past and are continuing now, or recently completed with a visible result.

Formula: have/has been + verb-ing. Example: She's been studying all day (and therefore tired). Focus on duration, not results.

el_u19.note: for 3 hours, since Monday, 2 years ago
el_u19.exp:
Unit 19 examines the use of since, for and ago in the context of time.

For indicates duration: for two hours, for a week. Since - starting point: since Monday, since 2020. Ago - with the Past Simple, counting backwards from the present: I saw him two days ago. Confusion since/for is one of the most common mistakes.

el_u20.text: Unit 20 – Present Perfect vs Past Simple
el_u20.note: I've seen it. / I saw it yesterday.
el_u20.exp:
Unit 20 explains the fundamental difference between Present Perfect and Past Simple.

Present Perfect - connection with the present, without indicating a specific time. Past Simple - a specific completed event in the past (indicating when). I've visited France (experience) vs. I visited France in 2019 (specific fact). From yesterday/last year/ago - only Past Simple.

el_u21.note: The door is opened. The car was stolen.
el_u21.exp:
Unit 21 introduces the passive voice for the present and past tenses.

Formula: am/is/are + V3 (Present) or was/were + V3 (Past). Used when the character is unknown or unimportant: My car was stolen last night. The character is introduced by the preposition by: The book was written by Tolstoy.

el_u22.text: Unit 22 - Passive voice in different tenses
el_u22.note: is made / was made / will be made
el_u22.exp:
Unit 22 expands the topic of the passive voice to different tenses and constructions.

The forms are analyzed: Present Simple Passive (is made), Past Simple Passive (was made), Future Passive (will be made), as well as Present Perfect Passive (has been made). Key principle: to be is consistent in time, V3 does not change.

el_u23.text: Unit 23 – Auxiliary verbs BE, DO, HAVE
el_u23.note: am doing / do you / have you
el_u23.exp:
Unit 23 systematizes the use of the three main auxiliary verbs of the English language.

Be - for Continuous and Passive; do/does/did - for questions and negatives in Simple; have/has/had - for Perfect tenses. The lesson teaches you how to choose the correct auxiliary verb depending on the grammatical structure.

el_u24.exp:
Unit 24 systematizes regular (Regular) and irregular (Irregular) verbs in English.

Regular ones add -ed to the infinitive for Past and Participle II. Incorrect ones need to be remembered in three forms (V1/V2/V3). The lesson contains a list of the most common irregular verbs with translation.

el_u25.text: Unit 25 – Present Continuous for the future
el_u25.exp:
Unit 25 shows that the Present Continuous can be used for planned actions in the future.

If we have a clear plan or agreement, we use the Continuous: I'm flying to London tomorrow. This is different from will (spontaneous decision) and going to (intention). A future tense marker (tomorrow, next week) is required.

el_u26.note: I'm going to study. She's going to rain.
el_u26.exp:
Unit 26 uses be going to to express intentions and obvious predictions.

Intentions: I'm going to learn Spanish this year (there is a plan). Predictions based on visible signs: Look at those clouds - it's going to rain! Formula: am/is/are + going to + infinitive.

el_u27.note: I'll help you. It will be sunny.
el_u27.exp:
Unit 27 introduces the future tense will + infinitive.

Used for spontaneous decisions (I'll open the window), promises (I'll call you), predictions without visible evidence (I think it will rain tomorrow). Short form: I'll, she'll, they'll. Denial: won't.

el_u28.note: Will you help? Shall I open the window?
el_u28.exp:
Unit 28 covers the modal verbs will and shall in detail.

Will is used for offers, promises and requests. Shall - mainly with I/we for offers of help: Shall I carry your bag? In modern language, shall is replaced by will, but remains in sentence questions.

el_u29.note: It might rain. She may be at home.
el_u29.exp:
Unit 29 explains the modal verbs might and may to express possibility.

Both convey the meaning of “maybe”: It might rain today (it might rain). May is a little more confident, might is a lesser degree of confidence. After them there is an infinitive without to: She may come. He might know

el_u30.note: I can swim. Could you help me?
el_u30.exp:
Unit 30 introduces can for abilities and permission, could for past abilities and polite requests.

Can I open the window? - permission. I could swim when I was five - past ability. Could you help me, please? is a polite request. After can/could there is an infinitive without to.

el_u31.note: You must stop. You needn't worry.
el_u31.exp:
Unit 31 explains the difference between must, mustn't and needn't.

Must is a necessity/obligation. Mustn't - a strict prohibition (You mustn't smoke here!). Needn't - lack of need (You needn't come - it is not necessary to come). Key difference: mustn't ≠ needn't!

el_u32.exp:
Unit 32 parses the modal verb should for advice and recommendations.

Should means “should, worth, recommended”: You should eat less sugar. Negation shouldn't - not worth it, should not: You shouldn't smoke. Should is not as categorical as must.

el_u33.note: I have to work. She has to leave.
el_u33.exp:
Unit 33 introduces the construction have to as an alternative to must for external obligations.

Must - the speaker himself considers something obligatory; have to - an obligation imposed by external circumstances: I have to wear a uniform at work. In questions and negatives, do/does is used: Do you have to work on Sundays?

el_u34.note: Would you like tea? I'd like to go.
el_u34.exp:
Unit 34 examines the use of would in polite sentences and wishes.

Would you like...? is a polite sentence (Would you like some coffee?). I'd like is a polite expression of desire (instead of I want). Also: Would you mind + -ing? Short form: I'd, she'd, they'd.

el_u35.note: Open the door! Don't be late!
el_u35.exp:
Unit 35 explores the imperative mood - the form for commands, requests and instructions.

Infinitive without a subject: Sit down! Be quiet! Come here!Negation: Don't + infinitive. Mitigation with please: Please close the door. / Close the door, please. Also: Let's go! (let us)

el_u36.note: I used to live there. She used to smoke.
el_u36.exp:
Unit 36 explores the construction used to + infinitive for actions that happened regularly in the past, but do not happen now.

I used to play football when I was a child (used to play, not now). Denial: I didn't use to like vegetables. Question: Did you use to...? There is no present tense form - only for the past.

el_u37.note: There is a book. There are two cats.
el_u37.exp:
Unit 37 explains the construction there is/are to describe the presence of something.

There is - singular or uncountable: There is a cat. There are - plural: There are two cats. Question: Is there a problem? Are there any questions? Denial: There isn't / There aren't.

el_u38.text: Unit 38 – There was / there will be / there has been
el_u38.note: There was a problem. Will there be...?
el_u38.exp:
Unit 38 extends the construction there is/are to other tenses.

There was/were (past), there will be (future), there has been / have been (Present Perfect). Examples:There was an accident. There will be a meeting tomorrow. There have been many changes.

el_u39.exp:
Unit 39 explores the use of the formal subject it to describe weather, time, distance and conditions.

Weather: It's raining. It's cold today.Time: It's 5 o'clock. It's Monday. Distance: It's 10 km to the city. Duration: It takes 30 minutes.

el_u40.text: Unit 40 - Short Answers with Auxiliary Verbs
el_u40.note: Yes, I do. / No, she isn't. / So do I.
el_u40.exp:
Unit 40 teaches you to give short answers to questions using an auxiliary verb.

Do you like coffee? - Yes, I do.Expressions of agreement: So do I / Neither do I. Short answers confirm or refute a question by repeating the auxiliary verb—you can't just say Yes or No in formal speech.

el_u41.note: It's nice, isn't it? You didn't go, did you?
el_u41.exp:
Unit 41 examines disjunctive questions - “tails” at the end of statements to confirm information.

Rule: statement + negative “tail” (and vice versa): She's a doctor, isn't she? They didn't come, did they?The “tail” repeats the auxiliary verb and the subject pronoun. With intonation: ↓ - rhetorical question, ↑ - real question.

el_u42.text: Unit 42 – Too, either, so, neither, nor
el_u42.note: I'm tired. So am I. / Me too.
el_u42.exp:
Unit 42 parses words to express agreement and add information.

Too / either - adding affirmative/negative. So + auxiliary + subject: I work hard. So does she. Neither/Nor + auxiliary + subject: I don't smoke. Neither does he.

el_u43.note: I'm not sure. They haven't arrived.
el_u43.exp:
Unit 43 systematizes the construction of negative sentences in all tenses.

Negation is built through not after the auxiliary verb. Summary table:am not / isn't / aren't; don't / doesn't / didn't; haven't / hasn't; won't; can'ts. Important: in Present/Past Simple, the auxiliary do/did is added specifically for negation.

el_u44.note: Why did she leave? Where are you going?
el_u44.exp:
Unit 44 explains the main rule of word order in English questions.

Order: Question word + auxiliary verb + subject + main verb. A typical mistake is direct word order: Why she left? → Why did she leave? The rule applies to all types of special questions.

el_u45.exp:
Unit 45 studies a special type of question where who or what replaces the subject.

When the question is to the subject, the auxiliary verb is not needed: Who broke the window? (not Who did break?). The verb is in the third person singular form: Who lives here? What makes you happy?

el_u46.note: Who did you talk to? What is it for?
el_u46.exp:
Unit 46 examines questions where a preposition is associated with a verb and is placed at the end of the sentence.

In English, the preposition usually remains with the verb: Who are you waiting for? (not For whom are you waiting? - this is formal). Examples:What are you looking at? Who did she go with?

el_u47.text: Unit 47 – Questions with what, which, how
el_u47.note: What color? Which one? How often?
el_u47.exp:
Unit 47 explains special questions with the question words what, which and various combinations with how.

What kind of...? What time...?. Which - choice from a limited number: Which dress do you prefer?. How + adjective/adverb: How far? How long? How often? How much? How many?

el_u48.text: Unit 48 – How long does it take?
el_u48.note: It takes 30 minutes to get there.
el_u48.exp:
Unit 48 teaches asking and answering about the duration of actions with the construction How long does it take?

Answers: It takes 20 minutes / an hour / two days. Construction with a subject: It takes me 30 minutes to walk to work. Past: How long did it take? It took three hours.

el_u49.note: Do you know where she lives?
el_u49.exp:
Unit 49 examines indirect questions (embedded questions) in subordinate clauses.

After introductory phrases (Do you know..., Can you tell me..., I wonder...), the word order is direct, without inversion: Do you know where she lives? (not where does she live?). Important: if/whether for yes/no questions: I don't know if he is coming.

el_u50.exp:
Unit 50 introduces indirect speech - retelling someone else's words.

When retelling, the tense of the verb “shifts back”: is → was, has done → had done, will → would. Examples: "I'm tired" → She said she was tired. "I'll call" → He said he would call. Pronouns and time indicators also change: today → that day, tomorrow → the next day.

el_u51.note: I want to go. I enjoy swimming.
el_u51.exp:
Unit 51 introduces a basic rule: some verbs require an infinitive with to, others require a gerund (-ing).

Infinitive: want to, need to, hope to, decide to. Gerund: enjoy, finish, stop, suggest, avoid. Some verbs combine with both: like to do / like doing (with a slight difference in meaning).

el_u52.note: He agreed to help. She loves reading.
el_u52.exp:
Unit 53 continues the theme by adding verbs that require -ing after prepositions, and constructions with an object before the infinitive.

After prepositions there is always a gerund: interested in doing, good at playing, thinking about going. Construction verb + object + infinitive: She asked me to help. He told me to wait.

el_u53.text: Unit 53 - Infinitive after make and let
el_u53.note: She made me cry. He let me go.
el_u53.exp: Unit 53 studies the verbs make and let, which require an infinitive without to after the object. to - as opposed to allow to, allow to, force to.

el_u54.note: I came here to learn. She works to earn money.
el_u54.exp:
Unit 54 shows that the infinitive with to can express the purpose or reason for an action (= in order to).

I went to the shop to buy milk. = in order to buy. It's a cost-effective way to explain why something is being done. Negative goal: in order not to, so as not to: She whispered so as not to wake him.

el_u55.text: Unit 55 – Verb go: go on, go for, go doing
el_u55.note: go shopping, go for a walk, go on holiday
el_u55.exp:
Unit 55 parses set expressions with the verb go.

Go + -ing for activities/outdoor activities: go swimming, go skiing, go shopping. Go for: go for a walk, go for a drink. Go on: go on holiday, go on a trip, go on a diet. These expressions need to be remembered as ready-made blocks.

el_u56.note: get tired, get married, get to work
el_u56.exp:
Unit 56 systematizes the ambiguous verb get and its basic meanings.

Get = receive: I got a letter. Get = become (change of state): get tired, get angry, get better. Get = get: get home, get to work. Phrasal verbs: get up, get on, get off, get back.

el_u57.text: Unit 57 - Difference between do and make
el_u57.exp:
Unit 57 explains the difference between do and make, which both translate as “to do”.

Do - for actions, tasks, work: do homework, do the dishes, do business. Make - for creation, production, result: make a cake, make a decision, make a mistake, make money. Many expressions you just need to remember.

el_u58.text: Unit 58 – The verb have in fixed expressions
el_u58.note: have breakfast, have a look, have fun
el_u58.exp:
Unit 58 breaks down the use of have in fixed expressions, where it means “to do an action”.

Have breakfast/lunch/dinner, have a shower, have a drink, have a party, have fun, have a look, have a rest, have a conversation. In these expressions, have is a semantic verb (not an auxiliary), so in the questions: Do you have breakfast every day?

el_u59.exp:
Unit 59 examines personal pronouns in two forms: nominative (subject) and objective (object).

Subject: I, you, he, she, it, we, they. Addition: me, you, him, her, it, us, them. The choice of form depends on the role in the sentence: She saw him (not She saw he).

el_u60.note: my book / the book is mine
el_u60.exp:
Unit 60 distinguishes between possessive adjectives (my, your, his, her) and possessive pronouns (mine, yours, his, hers).

Adjectives come before a noun: my car. Pronouns replace the noun: The car is mine. The form its is without an apostrophe (≠ it's = it is).

el_u61.exp:
Unit 61 tabulates all forms of pronouns: nominative, objective, possessive adjective, possessive pronoun and reflexives.

Complete system: I – me – my – mine – myself; he – him – his – his – himself; she – her – her – hers – herself, etc. Knowledge of all forms is necessary for the correct construction of sentences.

el_u62.exp:
Unit 62 reinforces all case forms of pronouns through exercises and examples from speech.

Emphasis on common errors: between you and I → between you and me; Her and me went → She and I went. Pronouns in complex constructions: It's me / It's him (conversational norm).

el_u63.exp:
Unit 63 studies reflexive pronouns, which are used when the subject and object are the same person.

I hurt myself. She looked at herself in the mirror. To enhance: I did it myself (= myself, without anyone’s help). Full series: myself, yourself, yourself, yourself, yourself, yourself, yourselves, yourself.

el_u64.note: Tom's car. The girl's name. My parents' house.
el_u64.exp:
Unit 64 examines the possessive case of nouns with an apostrophe.

Singular: Tom's, the girl's, my friend's. Plural (correct): my parents' house, the students' books. Incorrect plural: children's toys, men's clothes. The construction the + …'s to designate establishments: at the doctor's, at the hairdresser's.

el_u65.note: a book, an apple, a university, an hour
el_u65.exp:
Unit 65 introduces the indefinite article a/an and rules for its selection.

A - before a consonant, an - before a vowel. ⚠️ The choice depends on the sound, not the letter: a university [j], an hour [aʊ]. Status: first mentioned item, one of many, profession: She is a doctor.

el_u66.exp:
Unit 66 looks at pluralizing nouns.

Basic rule: add -s. Exceptions: -es after -s, -sh, -ch, -x; -ies (city→cities); incorrect: man→men, woman→women, child→children, tooth→teeth, sheep→sheep, fish→fish. Uncountable plural nouns. don't have.

el_u67.exp:
Unit 67 explains the concept of uncountable nouns that do not have a plural and are not used with a/an.

Examples: water, music, advice, information, news, money, furniture, luggage. To indicate a portion: a piece of advice, a glass of water, a bit of information. Common mistake: an advice, informations.

el_u68.note: some water, some advice, some music
el_u68.exp:
Unit 68 teaches you to use some with uncountable and plural nouns. number.

Some is used in statements: I need some help. There's some milk. In questions-suggestions and requests: Would you like some coffee? In denials and ordinary questions - any: Is there any milk? There isn't any

el_u69.note: I saw a dog. The dog was brown.
el_u69.exp:
Unit 69 explains the fundamental difference between the indefinite a and the definite the.

A - first mention or one of many. The - the subject is already known to the listener, one of a kind. I bought a book. The book is very interesting.There are no articles in the Russian language, so this is one of the most difficult topics for Russian speakers.

el_u70.note: the sun, the airport, the same, play the piano
el_u70.exp:
Unit 70 examines in detail the use of the article the.

The is used: before the only objects in the world (the sun, the moon, the Earth); with nouns specified by context; after same, first, next, last, only; with musical instruments (play the guitar); with geographical names (oceans, rivers, mountain ranges).

el_u71.text: Unit 71 – The: when you need it and when you don’t
el_u71.note: go to school / go to the cinema
el_u71.exp:
Unit 71 explains when the article the is dropped before place names.

Without the article when the place is used for its intended purpose: go to school/work/church/hospital. With the, when visiting for another reason: go to the school (to pick up a child). Also without an article: languages ​​(English), meals (have breakfast), sports (play football).

el_u72.note: the radio / television / phone / the bus
el_u72.exp:
Unit 72 parses set expressions with and without the in everyday speech.

With the: listen to the radio, on the phone, the internet, the cinema, the theatre. Without the: watch television/TV, at home, in bed, by car/bus/train. Some expressions go against the general rules and require memorization.

el_u73.note: the UK, the Thames, France, London
el_u73.exp:
Unit 73 systematizes the rules for using the with geographical names.

With the: rivers (the Thames), oceans (the Pacific), mountain ranges (the Alps), plural countries. or with "Republic/Kingdom" (the USA, the UK). Without the: cities (Paris), countries in singular. (France), continents (Europe), individual mountains (Mount Everest).

el_u74.note: this book / these books / that car / those cars
el_u74.exp:
Unit 74 explores demonstrative pronouns and their use in relation to number and distance.

This/these - close (here). That/those - far (there). Singular/plural: this→these, that→those. In telephone conversations:This is Anna speaking. Is that Tom?

el_u75.note: Which one? I want the blue one.
el_u75.exp:
Unit 75 parses one/ones to replace the referenced noun.

One replaces the singular count noun: I don't like this jacket. I prefer the red one. Ones - for plural: These shoes are nice, but the blue ones are cheaper. After possessives: His car is newer than mine/my old one.

el_u76.exp:
Unit 76 examines in detail the rules for using some and any.

Some - in statements and sentence questions. Any - in denials and questions. Derivatives: somebody/anyone, something/anything, somewhere/anywhere. Exception: any in statements = “any”: You can choose any color you like.

el_u77.note: There is no milk. None of them came.
el_u77.exp:
Unit 77 explains the difference between no, none and not any.

No + noun: There is no milk = There isn't any milk. None - no noun (replacement): How much money? None / None of us knew. ⚠️ In English there is one negation in a sentence: I don't know nothing → I don't know anything.

el_u78.note: Nobody came. I didn't see anybody.
el_u78.exp:
Unit 78 examines negative indefinite pronouns nobody/no one, nothing, nowhere and their alternatives with not.

Nobody = not anybody. Nothing = not anything. Nowhere = not anywhere. After nobody/nothing/nowhere the verb is affirmative: Nobody knows (not Nobody doesn't know). Agreement with they: Nobody came, did they?

el_u79.note: Is somebody there? Anybody can do this.
el_u79.exp:
Unit 79 reinforces the system of indefinite pronouns with -body/-one/-thing/-where.

In statements: someone, something, somewhere. In questions/negations: anyone, anything, anywhere. When any- in a statement = “any”: Anyone can join - anyone can join.

el_u80.exp:
Unit 80 explains the difference between every and all.

Every - each individually (singular): every student, every day. All - all together: all students, all day. Derivations from every: everyone/everybody, everything, everywhere. Every with units verb: Every student has a book.

el_u81.text: Unit 81 - All, most, some, any, no
el_u81.note: All cats / Most people / Some water / No idea
el_u81.exp:
Unit 81 organizes quantitative pronouns and their place in sentences.

Without of: general statements - All birds can fly. Most people work. With of: before a pronoun or definite article - All of them, most of the students, some of us. No always before a noun without an article: No information is available.

el_u82.note: Both sisters / Either day / Neither option
el_u82.exp:
Unit 82 examines the pronouns both, either, neither for two objects or people.

Both - both: Both sisters are tall. I like both. Either - either of the two: You can take either road. Neither - neither one nor the other: Neither answer is correct. Neither...nor / either...or - paired unions.

el_u83.text: Unit 83 – Much, many, a lot of
el_u83.note: much water / many friends / a lot of work
el_u83.exp:
Unit 83 explains the difference between much, many and a lot of.

Much - with uncountables: much money, much time. Many - with countable plurals: many books, many people. A lot of / lots of - with both types, more often in statements: a lot of work, a lot of friends. Much/many more often in questions and negations.

el_u84.note: few friends / little time / a few things
el_u84.exp:
Unit 84 parses few/little (little) and a few / a little (a little, enough).

Few - few, almost none (pessimistic): few friends. A few - several, a little (optimistic): a few friends. Similarly for uncountables: little time (little) vs. a little time (a little). The key difference is the attitude of the speaker.

el_u85.note: a beautiful day / the sky is blue
el_u85.exp:
Unit 85 introduces adjectives - words that describe nouns.

Position: before a noun (a big house) or after a linking verb (The house is big). Adjectives in English do not change according to gender, number and case. The order of several adjectives is: opinion → size → age → color → origin → material.

el_u86.exp:
Unit 86 explains the formation and use of adverbs of manner.

Most adverbs are formed by adding -ly to the adjective: slow→slowly, careful→carefully. Exceptions: good→well, fast→fast, hard→hard, early→early. An adverb answers the question “how?” and describes a verb, adjective or other adverb.

el_u87.exp:
Unit 87 teaches how to form the comparative degree of adjectives.

Short (1 syllable): +er: old→older, big→bigger. Long (2+ syllables): more + adjective: more expensive, more interesting. Exceptions: good→better, bad→worse, far→farther/further. The rules for doubling a consonant and changing y→i apply as in the Past Simple.

el_u88.note: She is taller than me. It's better than I thought.
el_u88.exp:
Unit 88 examines the use of than in comparison.

She is older than me / than I am. After than the object pronoun is used in colloquial speech: taller than me. Gain: much/far/a lot older than, a bit/slightly younger than.

el_u89.note: I'm not as tall as you.
el_u89.exp:
Unit 89 teaches you to express equality and inequality using as ... as.

As ... as - equality: She is as tall as her brother. Not as ... as - inequality (softer than than): This hotel isn't as expensive as that one. Comparison: not as tall as = shorter than. Colloquial version: not so... as.

el_u90.note: the oldest, the most expensive, the best
el_u90.exp:
Unit 90 explains the formation and use of superlative adjectives.

Short: the + adj. + est: the oldest, the biggest. Long: the most + adj.: the most beautiful. Always with the! Exceptions: the best, the worst, the furthest. After the excellent: the tallest student in the class, the most interesting book I've ever read.

el_u91.note: good enough / enough money / old enough to drive
el_u91.exp:
Unit 91 explores the word enough in different positions.

After an adjective/adverb: fast enough, old enough. Before a noun: enough money, enough time. Construction: adj/adv + enough + to-infinitive: She's old enough to drive. Is it warm enough to swim? Negation: not … enough.

el_u92.note: too tired to work / too much / too many
el_u92.exp:
