Unit 92 examines the word too (too) in combination with adjectives and adverbs.

Too + adjective/adverb - negative connotation (excessively): It's too cold to swim. Construction: too + adj + to-infinitive: She's too young to drive. Too much (uncountable) + too many (countable plural). ⚠️ Too ≠ also/as well.

el_u93.text: Unit 93 - Word Order in a Sentence
el_u93.note: She always works hard at the office.
el_u93.exp:
Unit 93 codifies the basic word order: subject → predicate → object → adverbial.

Adverbials of place and time are usually placed at the end: She works in London every day. You cannot put an adverbial adverb between a verb and a direct object: She reads every day books → She reads books every day.

el_u94.text: Unit 94 – Place of adverbs of frequency
el_u94.exp:
Unit 94 explains the rule for placing adverbs of frequency in a sentence.

Place: before the semantic verb, but after the auxiliary/to be: I usually get up early. She is always late. He doesn't often cook.Adverbs of frequency in descending order: always → usually → often → sometimes → rarely → never.

el_u95.note: I'm still waiting. She's already left.
el_u95.exp:
Unit 95 examines the adverbs still, yet, already and their place in the sentence.

Still - continues (expected ending): I'm still waiting. Place: before the verb or after be. Already - has already happened (faster than expected): standing in the middle. Yet - not yet / already?: at the end of questions and denials.

el_u96.text: Unit 96 – Give, lend, pass, send, show + two additions
el_u96.note: Give me the book. / Give the book to me.
el_u96.exp:
Unit 96 teaches how to construct sentences with two objects: direct and indirect.

Two orders: Give me the book (to whom first, without a preposition) = Give the book to me (preposition to). With pronouns, the second option is preferable: Give it to me (not Give me it). Verbs: give, lend, pass, send, show, tell, teach, offer.

el_u97.text: Unit 97 - And, but, or, so, because
el_u97.note: I was tired, so I went to bed.
el_u97.exp:
Unit 97 looks at basic coordinating conjunctions for connecting parts of a sentence.

And - addition. But is a contrast. Or is an alternative. So - consequence: It was late, so I left. Because - reason: I left because it was late. Each of these conjunctions in a compound sentence can be preceded by a comma.

el_u98.text: Unit 98 - When and null conditional type (if)
el_u98.note: When it rains, I stay home. If you heat ice, it melts.
el_u98.exp:
Unit 98 explores the conjunction when in complex sentences and introduces the Zero Conditional.

When + Present Simple → Present/Future Simple. Zero conditional (always true): If you heat water to 100°C, it boils. In subordinate conditions and time, we never use the future tense: when I will arrive → when I arrive.

el_u99.note: If it rains, I'll stay home.
el_u99.exp:
Unit 99 introduces the first type of conditional sentences for real and probable future situations.

Structure: If + Present Simple, will + infinitive. If it rains tomorrow, I won't go. The clause can come first or second: I'll take an umbrella if it rains. In the clause - never will: If it will rain.

int_u01.note: I am doing / She is working right now
int_u01.exp:
Unit 1 examines the Present Continuous in detail at the Intermediate level.

Three cases of use: action right now (I'm reading); temporary situation (She's staying with us this week); planned future (We're meeting tomorrow). Exceptional verbs (stative verbs) are not used in the Continuous: know, believe, want, need, like.

int_u02.note: I work / She works / general truths
int_u02.exp:
Unit 2 looks at the Present Simple at an advanced level: constant facts, habits, schedules.

Three key contexts: regular actions (I go to the gym twice a week); constant truths (The Earth revolves around the Sun); schedule and programs (The train leaves at 9). Difference with Continuous: constant vs. temporary.

int_u03.text: Unit 3 – Present Simple or Present Continuous
int_u03.note: I work here. / I'm working from home this week.
int_u03.exp:
Unit 3 compares two present tenses and teaches you to choose between them.

Present Simple - constant, usual, regular. Present Continuous - temporary, happening now or around this period. The key question is: is this business as usual or is it something temporary/happening now? She lives in Paris vs. She's living in Paris for a year.

int_u04.text: Unit 4 - Stative verbs: verbs not in Continuous
int_u04.note: I know / I understand / I want (not: I am knowing)
int_u04.exp:
Unit 4 takes a detailed look at stative verbs, which are rarely or never put into the Continuous.

Main groups: feelings (love, hate, like, prefer); perception (see, hear, smell, taste); mental activity (know, believe, understand, think); possession (have, own, belong). Some have two meanings: I think he's right (opinion) vs. I'm thinking about you (process).

int_u05.note: I worked / Did you go? / I didn't see
int_u05.exp:
Unit 5 systematizes the Past Simple at the Intermediate level.

Used for completed actions in the past indicating time. Regular verbs: +ed. Incorrect: V2 forms. Questions and denials - with did/didn't. List of the most common irregular verbs: go→went, see→saw, come→came, take→took, make→made.

int_u06.note: I was reading when you called.
int_u06.exp:
Unit 6 examines the Past Continuous in the context of its interaction with the Past Simple.

Past Continuous is a background ongoing action (I was cooking); Past Simple - interrupting short event (when the phone rang). Parallel ongoing actions: While she was studying, he was sleeping. Incompleteness: I was reading the book (not the fact of completion).

int_u07.note: I have finished. She has left.
int_u07.exp:
Unit 7 introduces the Present Perfect - a tense for past events that are relevant in the present.

Formula: have/has + V3. Use: when the result is important (I've broken my leg - the leg is broken now); lifetime experience (Have you ever...?); changing the situation (The situation has changed). It is impossible with a specific past tense: I have seen him yesterday.

int_u08.exp:
Unit 8 continues the theme of the Present Perfect, focusing on typical markers.

Just - just now. Already - already (surprise, earlier than expected). Yet - yet/already (in questions and negations). Ever - someday (in questions). Never - never. The position of these words in a sentence is strictly regulated.

int_u09.exp:
Unit 9 studies the Present Perfect Continuous: an action that began in the past and continues (or recently ended).

Formula: have/has been + -ing. Emphasis on the duration of the process: She's been studying for three hours. Visible result of a recent action: You've been running — you're out of breath!

int_u10.text: Unit 10 – Present Perfect vs Present Perfect Continuous
int_u10.exp:
Unit 10 compares two “perfect” tenses: result vs. process.

Present Perfect - emphasis on completeness and result: I've read three chapters. Present Perfect Continuous - emphasis on the duration of the process: I've been reading for two hours. With the verbs live, work, study, both options are often interchangeable.

int_u100.exp:
Unit 100 systematizes the formation of adverbs and the difference between adjectives and adjectives. adverb.

Most: adjective + -ly. Exceptions: good→well, fast→fast, hard→hard, early→early. Hardly ≠ hard: I work hard / I can hardly move. Late/lately: different values. The position of the adverb of manner is after the object.

int_u101.text: Unit 101 – Adjectives and adverbs: well, fast, late, hard
int_u101.note: It's a fast car. She drives fast.
int_u101.exp:
Unit 101 examines adverb adjectives with the same form and pairs with different meanings.

Fast, hard, late, early, high, low - adjective = adverb. Pairs: hard (hard) vs. hardly (barely); late (late) vs. lately (lately); high (high) vs. highly (very); near vs. nearly (almost).

int_u102.note: so cold / such cold weather / so many
int_u102.exp:
Unit 102 explains the difference between so and such in intensifying constructions.

So + adjective/adverb: so cold, so quickly, so much. Such + (a) + adjective + noun: such a cold day, such good food. Result constructions: so ... that, such ... that: It was so cold that we left early.

int_u103.note: too tired to work / not warm enough
int_u103.exp:
Unit 103 goes into detail about enough (enough) and too (too).

Enough after an adjective/adverb, before a noun: fast enough, enough money. Construction: adj + enough + to-infinitive. Too before an adjective/adverb: too tired, too quickly. Construction: too + adj + to-infinitive: too young to vote.

int_u104.note: quite good / rather difficult / fairly easy
int_u104.exp:
Unit 104 examines adverbs of moderate intensity and their nuances.

Quite (quite): quite good. With some adjectives = completely: quite right, quite wrong. Rather - more than expected, often with a negative connotation: rather difficult. Fairly - neutral, not very good. Pretty - colloquial = quite.

int_u105.exp:
Unit 105 examines comparative degree formation at the Intermediate level.

Short (1 syllable): +er. Two-syllables with -y: happy→happier. Long: more +. Exceptions: good→better, bad→worse, far→farther/further, old→older/elder. Doubling a consonant: big→bigger, hot→hotter.

int_u106.note: much better / a bit warmer / any better?
int_u106.exp:
Unit 106 shows the strengthening and weakening of the comparative degree.

Strengthening: much, far, a lot + comparative: much better, far more expensive. A small degree: a bit, a little, slightly: a bit warmer. With questions/denials:any: Is it any better? It's not any warmer. Progress design: getting better and better.

int_u107.note: as fast as / faster than / not as good as
int_u107.exp:
Unit 107 systematizes all comparative constructions.

As ... as - equality: She's as tall as her brother. Not as ... as - inequality (softer): It's not as good as I expected. Than after the comparative: She runs faster than me. After than/as - object form in colloquial speech: than him, as her.

int_u108.note: the oldest / the most interesting / the best
int_u108.exp:
Unit 108 studies superlatives at the Intermediate level.

Always with the: the oldest, the most beautiful, the best, the worst. After the superlative usually in (for place/group) or of (for time): the best player in the team, the hottest day of the year. With Present Perfect: It's the best film I've ever seen.

int_u109.text: Unit 109 - Word Order: Place and Time (Lesson 1)
int_u109.note: She works in London every day.
int_u109.exp:
Unit 109 explains the order of place and time adverbs at the end of a sentence.

General rule: verb + object + place + time: She went to the cinema last night. Not allowed: She went last night to the cinema. A few circumstances of the place: from smallest to largest: I live at 12 Oxford Street in London in England.

int_u11.text: Unit 11 – How long have you been...?
int_u11.note: How long have you been waiting?
int_u11.exp:
Unit 11 teaches you to ask questions about the duration of current situations with the construction How long...?

With Present Perfect/Continuous: How long have you been living here? How long have they been married? Answers with for and since: I've been here for two years / since 2022. Difference: for is the duration, since is the starting point.

int_u110.text: Unit 110 - Word Order: Adverbs of Verbs (Lesson 2)
int_u110.note: always, often, usually - place in a sentence
int_u110.exp:
Unit 110 examines in detail the position of adverbs of frequency and other adverbs on verbs.

Before the semantic verb: I always eat breakfast. After the auxiliary: She is often late. I have never been there. Probably, definitely, certainly, obviously - the same position. Adverb of manner: She speaks English well (at the end).

int_u111.text: Unit 111 – Still, yet, already; any more / no longer
int_u111.note: I'm still waiting. He no longer works here.
int_u111.exp:
Unit 111 systematizes adverbs associated with the change/continuation of a situation.

Still - continues (despite the expected ending). Yet - not yet/already (in questions and negations). Already - has already happened. Any more / any longer / no longer - the situation has stopped: He doesn't work here any more = He no longer works here.

int_u112.note: Even I knew. She can't even walk.
int_u112.exp:
Unit 112 parses the focusing adverb even to express surprise or intensification.

Position: before the word/group on which the intensification falls. Even I knew the answer (even me). She can't even walk (even walk). Even + comparative: It's even better than I expected. Negative reinforcement: not even: He didn't even say sorry.

int_u113.text: Unit 113 – Although, though, even though, in spite of, despite
int_u113.note: Although she was tired, she worked.
int_u113.exp:
Unit 113 explores ways to express concession (opposition).

Although/though/even though - conjunctions (+ subordinate clause): Although it was cold, she went out. In spite of/despite - prepositions (+ noun/-ing): Despite being tired, she worked. In spite of the rain, they continued.

int_u114.note: Take an umbrella in case it rains.
int_u114.exp:
Unit 114 examines the construction in case - “in case.”

In case ≠ if: in case suggests precautions before the event: I'll call you in case I'm late (I'll call in advance). If is a condition. After in case - Present Simple or Past Simple, never will/would. Just in case - colloquial.

int_u115.text: Unit 115 — Unless, as long as, provided, providing
int_u115.note: Unless you hurry, you'll be late.
int_u115.exp:
Unit 115 studies conditional conjunctions-alternatives if.

Unless = if...not: Unless you hurry = If you don't hurry. As long as / provided (that) / providing (that) - a condition, subject to which: You can borrow my car as long as you're careful.After all these conjunctions, the present tense (as in if-clauses).

int_u116.note: As I was leaving, the phone rank.
int_u116.exp:
Unit 116 parses the polysemous conjunction as in the meanings of “because” and “while”.

As = because: As it was late, I went home. As = when/while (simultaneously): As she was walking, she saw an accident. Difference: in meaning “when” - only with ongoing actions. Just as - at that very moment.

int_u117.text: Unit 117 – Like and as: such as
int_u117.note: She looks like her mother. She works as a teacher.
int_u117.exp:
Unit 117 distinguishes between like (preposition) and as (conjunction/preposition) in the meaning of comparison.

Like (preposition) + noun/pronoun: She sings like a bird. He's like his father. As (conjunction) + sentence or role: Do as I say. She works as a manager. Typical error: Do like I do → Do as I do.

int_u118.text: Unit 118 – Like / as if / as though: as if
int_u118.note: He looks as if he's seen a ghost.
int_u118.exp:
Unit 118 examines like/as if/as though constructions to describe impressions.

As if / as though - conjunctions (+ subordinate clause). Like is a conversational option. Unreal situations: past tense: She talks as if she knew everything (but doesn’t know). Real: present: It looks as if it's going to rain.

int_u119.note: for three days / during the film / while I was there
int_u119.exp:
Unit 119 delineates for, during, while to indicate a period of time.

For - duration: for two hours. During (preposition) + noun: during the film. While (conjunction) + sentence: while I was watching. Key: during before a noun, while before a clause.

int_u12.text: Unit 12 – Present Perfect with since and for
int_u12.note: I've known her for years. Since Monday.
int_u12.exp:
Unit 12 goes into detail about using since and for with Perfect tenses.

For + period: for two hours, for a week, for ages. Since + moment: since 8 o'clock, since Monday, since I was a child. After since there can be a whole subordinate clause: since we last met. In negative: I haven't seen him for years.

int_u120.text: Unit 120 - By, until, by the time
int_u120.note: I'll finish by Friday. By the time she arrived…
int_u120.exp:
Unit 120 compares by and until as temporal prepositions.

Until - up to the moment (continuous): I waited until 9pm. By - no later than (end point): I'll be home by 10. By the time + subordinate clause: By the time she arrived, I had already left. With Past Perfect: the action ended BEFORE the past event.

int_u121.text: Unit 121 – Prepositions of time: at, on, in
int_u121.note: at 5 / on Monday / in July / in 2020
int_u121.exp:
Unit 121 systematizes prepositions of time.

At: exact time (at 8 o'clock), holidays (at Christmas), night (at night), “at the weekend”. On: days of the week, dates (on Monday, on 5th May). In: months, years, seasons, parts of the day (in the morning). Without preposition: this, next, last, every.

int_u122.text: Unit 122 - On time and in time; at the end and in the end
int_u122.note: on time (on time) / in time (in time)
int_u122.exp:
Unit 122 sorts out frequently confused pairs of prepositional expressions.

On time - exactly on time, on schedule. In time - early enough, in time: We arrived in time for the show. At the end - at the end of something specific: at the end of the film. In the end - ultimately, in the end.

int_u123.text: Unit 123 – Prepositions of place: in, at, on (lesson 1)
int_u123.note: in the room / at the bus stop / on the table
int_u123.exp:
Unit 123 at the Intermediate level examines in detail in, at, on as prepositions of place.

In - inside a closed space: in the room, in a box. On - on a surface or line: on the table, on the wall, on the right. At - at the point: at the door, at the top of the stairs. Comparison: in the corner (inside the corner) vs. at the corner (at the corner outside).

int_u124.text: Unit 124 – Prepositions of place: in, at, on (lesson 2)
int_u124.note: in hospital / at home / on TV / on the bus
int_u124.exp:
Unit 124 examines set expressions with prepositions of place.

In: in hospital, in prison, in the city centre. At: at home, at work, at school, at the station. On: on the left/right, on the coast, on TV, on the bus/train. Numbers on the page/floor: on page 5, on the second floor.

int_u125.text: Unit 125 – Prepositions of place: in, at, on (lesson 3)
int_u125.note: in the front / at the front / on the front
int_u125.exp:
Unit 125 continues the theme by looking at subtle differences in the use of prepositions in similar contexts.

Similes: in the front (of the car) - inside. At the front (of the building) - at the front outside. On the front (page) - on the surface. Similar cases with back, side, top, bottom, end.

int_u126.text: Unit 126 – Prepositions of direction: to, at, in, into
int_u126.note: go to / arrive at / look at / come in
int_u126.exp:
Unit 126 examines prepositions indicating the direction of movement.

To - direction of the goal: go to school. Into - movement inward: She walked into the room. At with look/throw verbs: look at, throw at, aim at. In vs. into: She swam in the lake (where) vs. She jumped into the lake (where).

int_u127.text: Unit 127 – On, in, at in different situations
int_u127.note: on a bus / in a taxi / at a party
int_u127.exp:
Unit 127 systematizes the use of on, in, at in situations where the choice is not obvious.

Transport: on a bus/train/plane/ship (large), in a car/taxi (small). Events: at a party, at a concert, at a meeting. Islands: in Majorca (large) vs. on a small island. Specific combinations need to be remembered.

int_u128.note: by car / by chance / by the time / by himself
int_u128.exp:
Unit 128 systematizes the ambiguous preposition by in different contexts.

Transport: by car, by bus, by train, by plane. The Liability Agent: written by Dickens. Method: by accident, by mistake, by credit card. Nearby: sit by the window. Loneliness: all by herself. Before the deadline: I'll finish by Friday.

int_u129.note: a reason for / a lack of / difficulty in
int_u129.exp:
Unit 129 examines stable combinations of nouns with prepositions.

Frequent combinations: a reason for, a lack of, a need for, an increase in, a decrease in, a demand for, difficulty in, interest in, experience of, contact with, reaction to. After the preposition there is a gerund: a reason for leaving, difficulty in understanding.

int_u13.text: Unit 13 – Present Perfect vs Past Simple (Part 1)
int_u13.note: Have you seen it? / Did you see it yesterday?
int_u13.exp:
Unit 13explains the fundamental difference between the two tenses with numerous examples.

Present Perfect - without indicating time, connection with the present. Past Simple - a specific past indicating when. I've lost my keys (still no keys) vs. I lost my keys yesterday (fact about the past). Since ago, yesterday, last year - only Past Simple.

int_u130.note: good at / afraid of / different from
int_u130.exp:
Unit 130 studies stable combinations of adjectives with prepositions.

With of: afraid of, ashamed of, aware of, capable of, fond of, proud of, tired of, typical of, jealous of. With at: good at, bad at, brilliant at, clever at. With from: different from, free from. With for: famous for, responsible for, ready for.

int_u131.note: interested in / keen on / satisfied with
int_u131.exp:
Unit 131 continues with adjectives + prepositions, covering in, on, with, to, about, by.

With in: interested in, involved in, successful in. With on: keen on, dependent on. With with: satisfied with, pleased with, bored with, angry with. With to: married to, similar to, used to, close to, opposed to.

int_u132.text: Unit 132 – Prepositions at and to after verbs
int_u132.note: look at / look to / aim at / throw at
int_u132.exp:
Unit 132 parses verbs that require at or to.

With at: look at, stare at, glance at, laugh at, point at, shout at, throw at, aim at, shoot at. With to: talk to, speak to, listen to, explain to, describe to, suggest to, write to. Difference: at - direction of gaze/action, to - communication.

int_u133.text: Unit 133 – Prepositions about, for, of, after
int_u133.note: worry about / wait for / dream of / look after
int_u133.exp:
Unit 133 studies verbs with the prepositions about, for, of, after.

With about: worry about, talk about, dream about, think about, complain about. With for: wait for, ask for, apply for, search for, vote for. With of: dream of, consists of, smell of. With after: look after, take after, ask after.

int_u134.text: Unit 134 – Prepositions about and of: hear, think, dream
int_u134.note: hear about / think about / remind of
int_u134.exp:
Unit 134 goes into detail about verbs that can be used with about or of.

Think about (think about, ponder) vs. think of (remember, count). Hear about (find out the news) vs. hear of (hear about existence). Dream about (dreaming) vs. dream of (dream). Remind somebody of: She reminds me of your sister.

int_u135.text: Unit 135 – Prepositions of, for, from, on
int_u135.note: approve of / suffer from / depend on
int_u135.exp:
Unit 135 parses verbs with the prepositions of, for, from, on.

With of: approve of, consist of, accuse of. With for: thank for, apologise for, blame for, pay for. With from: suffer from, recover from, borrow from, differ from, prevent from. With on: depend on, concentrate on, insist on, rely on.

int_u136.text: Unit 136 – Prepositions in, into, with, to, on
int_u136.note: believe in / crash into / succeed in
int_u136.exp:
Unit 136 completes the series on prepositions with verbs: in, into, with, to, on.

With in: believe in, invest in, succeed in, specialise in, result in. With into: divide into, crash into, translate into. With with: agree with, argue with, deal with, discuss with. With to: belong to, react to, refer to.

int_u137.note: get up / turn on / look after - principles
int_u137.exp:
Unit 137 systematizes the concept of a phrasal verb and the principles of its use.

Phrasal verb = verb + postposition (particle or preposition), the meaning is often unpredictable. Separate/inseparable: turn off the TV / turn the TV off (separate), but look after the children (inseparable). The pronoun is always between parts: turn it off.

int_u138.text: Unit 138 – Phrasal verbs with in and out
int_u138.note: fill in / give out / find out / break in
int_u138.exp:
Unit 138 parses phrasal verbs with postpositions in and out.

In: fill in (fill), drop in (enter), break in (hack), join in (join). Out: find out (find out), give out (distribute), run out of (run out), point out (specify). The meaning of out often = completeness.

int_u139.note: carry out / sort out / turn out / wear out
int_u139.exp:
Unit 139 continues phrasal verbs with out.

Carry out - execute. Sort out - figure it out. Turn out - to turn out. Wear out - wear out. Work out - decide/train/get it. Cut out - cut out. Leave out - skip. The meaning of each needs to be taught with examples.

int_u14.text: Unit 14 – Present Perfect vs Past Simple (Part 2)
int_u14.note: It's the first time I've done this.
int_u14.exp:
Unit 14 continues the comparison, looking at subtle cases and typical contexts.

Present Perfect with It's the first time…, It's the best…I've ever…. The difference is in the accent: Did you have a good holiday? (vacation is over) vs. Have you had a good holiday? (context now). News - Present Perfect, details - Past Simple.

int_u140.text: Unit 140 – Phrasal verbs with on and off (lesson 1)
int_u140.note: put on / take off / set off / call off
int_u140.exp:
Unit 140 studies phrasal verbs with on and off.

On: put on (put on), go on (continue), carry on (continue), turn on (turn on). Off: take off (take off; take off), call off (cancel), set off (go), put off (put off).

int_u141.text: Unit 141 – Phrasal verbs with on and off (lesson 2)
int_u141.note: fall off / get on / switch off / run off
int_u141.exp:
Unit 141 continues on/off phrasal verbs with new examples.

Get on - get on transport / get along. Get off - get out of the vehicle. Fall off - fall from. Switch off/on - turn off/on. Show off - to show off. Go off - go bad / go off (about an alarm). Pay off - justify yourself.

int_u142.text: Unit 142 – Phrasal verbs with up and down
int_u142.note: grow up / break down / slow down / set up
int_u142.exp:
Unit 142 parses phrasal verbs with up and down.

Up often = increase, completeness: grow up, save up, speed up, clean up. Down often = reduction, termination: break down (break down), slow down (slow down), turn down (refuse / reduce), calm down (calm down).

int_u143.note: pick up / give up / make up / keep up
int_u143.exp:
Unit 143 focuses on phrasal verbs with up.

Pick up - pick up, pick up. Give up - give up, give up. Make up - invent / put up with. Keep up (with) - keep up. End up - end up in the end. Put up (with) - endure. Take up - take up (a hobby). Use up - use everything.

int_u144.note: turn up / show up / come up with / look up
int_u144.exp:
Unit 144 continues up verbs with new vocabulary.

Turn up - appear. Show up - appear. Come up with - come up with. Look up - search in the dictionary. Set up - to establish, organize. Bring up - bring up / raise a topic. Split up - to part. Wake up - wake up.

int_u145.text: Unit 145 – Phrasal verbs with away and back
int_u145.note: go away / throw away / come back / look back
int_u145.exp:
Unit 145 completes the series on phrasal verbs: away and back.

Away: go away (to leave), throw away (throw away), run away (to run away), give away (to give away), get away (to slip away), put away (remove). Back: come back (return), look back (look back), pay back (repay the debt), give back (return).

int_u15.note: She had already left when I arrived.
int_u15.exp:
Unit 15 introduces the Past Perfect - “past before the past.”

Formula: had + V3. Used when you need to show that one past action happened before another: When I arrived, she had already left. Without indicating the sequence, the Past Perfect is not needed: I had breakfast and went to work.

int_u16.note: I had been waiting for an hour before she arrived.
int_u16.exp:
Unit 16 examines the Past Perfect Continuous - the ongoing action that preceded the past moment.

Formula: had been + -ing. Emphasis on the duration of the action until a certain past moment: She was exhausted because she had been working all day. Similar to the Present Perfect Continuous, but shifted even further into the past.

int_u17.note: Have you got a car? / Do you have a car?
int_u17.exp:
Unit 17 compares have got (British) and have (American) in the meaning of possession.

Both express the same thing: I've got a problem = I have a problem. The difference in the formation of questions and negatives: Have you got...? / I haven't got…vs. Do you have...? / I don't have... In the past - only had (without got).

int_u18.note: I used to smoke but I stopped.
int_u18.exp:
Unit 18 examines the construction used to + infinitive for past habits and states.

Used to describes something that was regular in the past, but has now changed: I used to live in Moscow. Negation: I didn't use to... Question: Did you use to...? ⚠️ No present form time! Not to be confused with I'm used to doing (used to...).

int_u19.text: Unit 19 – Present Continuous and Simple for the future
int_u19.note: I'm meeting her tomorrow. The train leaves at 8.
int_u19.exp:
Unit 19 explains the use of present tenses for the future.

Present Continuous - for planned personal actions: We're going to Paris next week. Present Simple - for schedules and programs beyond our control: The film starts at 7. My flight leaves tomorrow morning.

int_u20.note: I'm going to study harder. It's going to snow.
int_u20.exp:
Unit 20 takes a closer look at be going to for intentions and predictions based on obvious signs.

Intentions (we already have a plan): I'm going to quit my job. Obvious predictions (see evidence): Look at the sky - it's going to rain! In the past: It was going to rain but it didn't

int_u21.note: I'll help you. It will be cold tomorrow.
int_u21.exp:
Unit 21 introduces will for spontaneous decisions, promises and predictions without evidence.

Spontaneous decision: The phone is ringing - I'll get it! Promise: I'll call you tonight. Prediction: I think it will be a great match. Short form: 'll. Denial: won't. Question with Shall I/we?

int_u22.note: Shall I open the window? Will you be there?
int_u22.exp:
Unit 22 deepens the topic of will: offers, requests, questions about intentions.

Offer of help: Shall I carry your bag? Request/question about desire: Will you have some coffee? Question about intention: Will you be at the party? Promise with future result: Don't worry - everything will be fine

int_u23.note: I'll do it. / I'm going to do it.
int_u23.exp:
Unit 23 compares will and going to: spontaneity vs. planning.

Will - decision at the moment of speech, prediction, promise. Going to - an already made decision/intention, a prediction with evidence. Example:Why did you buy flour? — I'm going to make a cake(already decided) vs. OK, I'll make tea (just decided).

int_u24.text: Unit 24 - Future Continuous and Future Perfect
int_u24.note: I'll be working at 8. I'll have finished by then.
int_u24.exp:
Unit 24 introduces two "advanced" future tenses.

Future Continuous (will be + -ing): the action will continue into the future: At 10 tomorrow I'll be sleeping. Future Perfect (will have + V3): the action will be completed by the future moment: By Friday I'll have finished the report.

int_u25.note: If it rains, I'll take an umbrella.
int_u25.exp:
Unit 25 examines the first type of conditional sentences for real future situations.

Structure: If + Present Simple, will + infinitive. The condition may be real or probable. In a subordinate clause - never will: If you come early, we'll have more time. Options: when, unless, as long as, provided (that) instead of if.

int_u26.text: Unit 26 - Can, could, be able to
int_u26.note: I can swim. I was able to escape. I'll be able to come.
int_u26.exp:
Unit 26 systematizes the expression of ability: can, could and be able to.

Can - ability now. Could - ability in the past (general): I could swim when I was five. Was/were able to - specific situation in the past: I was able to escape from the fire. Be able to - the only form for the future and Perfect: I'll be able to, I've been able to.

int_u27.note: Could you help? I could be wrong.
int_u27.exp:
Unit 27 examines could not only as the past can, but also in other meanings.

Polite request: Could you help me, please? Theoretical possibility: It could rain tomorrow. Sentence: We could go to the cinema. Couldn't - impossibility: She couldn't have done it — she was away.

int_u28.note: She must be tired. He can't be serious.
int_u28.exp:
Unit 28 explains must and can't for logical conclusions about the present.

Must - confident positive logic: You've been traveling all day - you must be exhausted. Can't - confident negative logic: That can't be true! It is not an obligation (like must work), but inference.

int_u29.note: It might rain. She may not come.
int_u29.exp:
Unit 29 introduces may and might to express possibility in the present and future.

Both convey uncertainty: “maybe.” May - slightly more likely (50%). Might - less confidence. After them there is an infinitive without to: He may know. She might be at home. Negation: may not / might not (without contraction mayn't).

int_u30.text: Unit 30 – May and might (lesson 2): the past
int_u30.note: She might have missed the train.
int_u30.exp:
Unit 30 examines may/might in relation to past events and situations.

May/might + have + V3 - conclusion about the past: She might have taken the wrong road. Contrast with must have (certainty) and can't have (impossibility): He must have arrived. She can't have forgotten.

int_u31.note: I must go. / I have to go.
int_u31.exp:
Unit 31 compares must (internal feeling of necessity) and have to (external requirement).

Must: the speaker himself considers it necessary: I must phone her tonight. Have to: rules, circumstances: I have to wear a uniform at work. In questions and negatives have to uses do/does/did. Must no form of the past → use had to.

int_u32.note: You must stop. You needn't worry.
int_u32.exp:
Unit 32 distinguishes between three closely related modal expressions.

Must - the need to do. Mustn't - impossible (prohibition): You mustn't drive without a license. Needn't - optional (lack of need): You needn't come - I'll manage alone. ⚠️ Mustn't ≠ needn't: prohibition vs. optional!

int_u33.exp:
Unit 33 studies should for advice, recommendations and opinions about what is right.

Should = “should”, “worth”: You should eat less sugar. Question about advice: What should I do? Regret about the past: You should have called (should have called, but didn’t call). Shouldn't - not worth it: You shouldn't smoke.

int_u34.note: She should be here by now.
int_u34.exp:
Unit 34 shows should in the sense of expected or expected.

Should as expectation: The package should arrive tomorrow (expected). Surprise from violation of expectations: Why should she be angry? Construction should have + V3: She should have finished by now (completion was expected, but...). Also in subordinate clauses: It's strange that he should say that.

int_u35.text: Unit 35 – It's time and you'd better
int_u35.note: It's time to go. You'd better hurry.
int_u35.exp:
Unit 35 examines the constructions it's time and had better.

It's time to do: it's time to do it. It's time you did: it's high time (past), with regret: It's time you went to bed. You'd better do: do better (advice with a warning about the consequences): You'd better hurry or you'll be late. 'd better = had better.

int_u36.note: Would you like...? I'd like to go. I would...
int_u36.exp:
Unit 36 systematizes the basic meanings of would.

Polite sentences: Would you like some tea? Desire (instead of want): I'd like to go to Japan. Regular past (= used to): When I was young, I would often go fishing. Imaginary: What would you do if...?

int_u37.exp:
Unit 37 teaches you to express requests with varying degrees of politeness.

Can you...? - a neutral request. Could you...? - more polite. Would you...? - polite or formal. Would you mind + -ing? - very polite: Would you mind opening the window? Answers: Of course! Sure! No problem. I'm afraid I can't

int_u38.text: Unit 38 - Conditional Type 1 and 2 (If I do / If I did)
int_u38.note: If I see her, I'll tell her. If I were rich…
int_u38.exp:
Unit 38 compares the first and second conditional types.

Type 1 (real): If I see her, I'll say hello. Type 2 (unreal/unlikely): If I won the lottery, I'd buy a house. The key difference is the probability of the situation. Were for all persons in Type 2: If I were you / If she were here.

int_u39.text: Unit 39 – Conditional type 2 and I wish
int_u39.note: I wish I knew the answer.
int_u39.exp:
Unit 39 connects Type 2 conditionals with the construction I wish for the unreal present.

I wish + Past Simple: the desire to change the current situation (I wish I had a car - I don’t have a car). I wish I were taller / smarter. Analogue: If only I knew. The forms are the same as in Type 2.

int_u40.text: Unit 40 - Conditional Type 3 (If I had done)
int_u40.note: If I had studied, I would have passed.
int_u40.exp:
Unit 40 explores the third type of conditional for unreal past situations.

Structure: If + Past Perfect, would have + V3. Subjunctive mood for the past: If I had arrived earlier, I would have met her. Mixed type: If you had listened (past), you would know (present).

int_u41.note: I wish I could fly. I wish he hadn't done that.
int_u41.exp:
Unit 41 systematizes all constructions with wish.

Wish + Past Simple - unreal present. Wish + Past Perfect - regret about the past: I wish I hadn't said that. Wish + would - desire for change (irritation or impatience): I wish it would stop raining. If only - the same, but with a greater emotional emphasis.

int_u42.note: The window was broken. Cheese is made from milk.
int_u42.exp:
Unit 42 introduces the passive voice: the action is more important than the performer.

Formula: to be + V3. Present Simple Passive: is made, are built. Past Simple Passive: was stolen, were invited. The doer (by-phrase) is used only when important: The Mona Lisa was painted by Leonardo.

int_u43.text: Unit 43 – Passive voice in all tenses (lesson 2)
int_u43.note: is being made / has been found / will be built
int_u43.exp:
Unit 43 extends the passive voice to all basic tenses.

Form table: is made / was made / has been made / is being made / was being made / will be made / has to be made. Rule: to be consistent with time, V3 unchanged. Continuous Passive: The road is being repaired.

int_u44.note: I was told. He was given a book.
int_u44.exp:
Unit 44 studies the passive voice of verbs with two objects.

The verbs give, tell, show, offer, ask can be passivized in different ways: She gave me a book → I was given a book / A book was given to me. It is more natural to put in the passive the one who is the subject: I was offered a job.

int_u45.text: Unit 45 – It is said… / He is said to be…
int_u45.note: It is said that he is rich. He is said to be rich.
int_u45.exp:
Unit 45 examines passive constructions for communicating information: It is said/reported/thought/believed…

Two options: It is said that he is a genius = He is said to be a genius. Other verbs: believe, consider, expect, know, report, think, understand. Past: He is said to have been rich.

int_u46.exp:
Unit 46 explores the construct have something done - when we organize work for others to do.

I had my hair cut (I had my hair cut). She's having her house painted. Comparison with the passive: passive - what they did to us; have smth done - what we organized to do. Also: get something done (colloquial version).

int_u47.note: She said she was tired. He told me he would come.
int_u47.exp:
Unit 47 systematizes the rules of indirect speech when retelling statements.

Tense shift: is → was, has done → had done, will → would, can → could. Pronouns change according to the subject of the retelling. Say vs. tell: She said (that)... / She told me (that)... - after tell the addressee must be indicated.

int_u48.text: Unit 48 - Reported Speech (Lesson 2): Questions and Orders
int_u48.note: He asked me where I lived. She told me to wait.
int_u48.exp:
Unit 48 examines indirect questions and orders/requests.

Indirect question - direct word order, without a question mark: He asked me where I worked. Yes/no questions: She asked if/whether I was ready. Indirect orders/requests: He told me to wait. She asked me not to say anything.

int_u49.note: Do you? Is she? Have they? Will it?
int_u49.exp:
Unit 49 systematizes the construction of general and specific questions in all tenses.

Inversion rule: auxiliary/modal verb + subject + main verb. The Present/Past Simple adds do/does/did. Special questions: question word + inversion:Where do you live? What has she done?

int_u50.text: Unit 50 - Questions in simple and complex sentences (lesson 2)
int_u50.note: Do you know where she lives?
int_u50.exp:
Unit 50 parses built-in (indirect) questions in complex sentences.

After introductory phrases (Do you know..., Can you tell me..., I wonder..., I'd like to know...) - direct word order without inversion: Do you know where she lives? (not where does she live?). With if/whether for general questions: I wonder if he is coming.

int_u51.text: Unit 51 - Auxiliary verbs in a sentence
int_u51.note: So do I. Neither can she. I do like it!
int_u51.exp:
Unit 51 examines the special functions of auxiliary verbs: short agreement, disagreement, strengthening.

So do I / Neither do I - short agreement. I do like it / She does know! - strengthening (emphatic do). Short answers:Yes, she has. / No, I didn't. Avoiding repetition: Can you swim? Yes, I can

int_u52.note: It's cold, isn't it? She didn't call, did she?
int_u52.exp:
Unit 52 explores question tags in detail.

Principle: affirmative sentence → negative tail (and vice versa). The tail repeats the auxiliary verb: She's a doctor, isn't she? Intonation: ↓ - confirmation of your thought, ↑ - real question. Special cases:I am, aren't I? Let's go, shall we?

int_u53.note: I enjoy swimming. She finished working.
int_u53.exp:
Unit 53 explores verbs followed by a gerund (-ing).

Verbs: enjoy, finish, stop, mind, avoid, suggest, recommend, admit, consider, deny, risk, imagine, practice. Example:I enjoy swimming. Have you finished eating? Rule: These verbs require a gerund, not an infinitive with to.

int_u54.note: I want to go. She decided to stay.
int_u54.exp:
Unit 54 examines verbs followed by an infinitive with to.

Verbs: want, need, hope, plan, decide, expect, manage, refuse, learn, offer, promise, seem, tend. Example:I want to go. She managed to escape. Construction with an object: She asked me to help.

int_u55.note: She told me to wait. He asked her not to go.
int_u55.exp:
Unit 55 parses the construction verb + object + to-infinitive.

Verbs: tell, ask, want, expect, allow, advise, encourage, order, remind, warn, help. Example:She told me to wait. He wants her to come. Denial: She asked me not to say anything.

int_u56.text: Unit 56 - Infinitive or Gerund: General Rule
int_u56.note: I like to swim / I like swimming - difference?
int_u56.exp:
Unit 56 explains when the infinitive and gerund are interchangeable and when they are not.

After like, love, hate, prefer: both are possible with a slight difference. Like doing - I like it as an activity. Like to do - I think it’s correct/useful. After begin, start, continue - both options make no difference. Key “only infinitive” verbs: want, need, hope.

int_u57.text: Unit 57 – Try, need, help: infinitive or gerund
int_u57.note: try doing vs. try to do / need cleaning
int_u57.exp:
Unit 57 parses verbs with two meanings depending on the form of the next verb.

Try to do - try to do (goal). Try doing - try as an option. Remember/forget to do - about the future. Remember/forget doing - about the past. Stop to do - stop in order to. Stop doing - stop. Need + -ing = passive meaning: The car needs washing.

int_u58.text: Unit 58 – Like / would like: infinitive or gerund
int_u58.note: I like swimming. / I'd like to swim.
int_u58.exp:
Unit 58 explains the fundamental difference between like and would like.

Like + -ing: Like as an activity in general. Would like + to-infinitive: a specific desire now or in the future (= want, but more polite). I like dancing ≠ I'd like to dance. Similarly: love / would love, prefer / would prefer.

int_u59.note: I prefer reading. I'd rather stay home.
int_u59.exp:
Unit 59 parses preference expressions with prefer and would rather.

Prefer + -ing to + -ing: general preference: I prefer reading to watching TV. Would prefer + to-infinitive: specific point. Would rather + infinitive without to: I'd rather stay home tonight. With subject: I'd rather you didn't tell her.

int_u60.exp:
Unit 60 enforces the rule that prepositions are always followed by a gerund.

Preposition + -ing: She's good at cooking. I'm interested in learning. Before leaving, check your email. Without + -ing: He left without saying goodbye. By + -ing = method: I lost weight by exercising regularly.

int_u61.note: I'm used to waking up early.
int_u61.exp:
Unit 61 examines be/get used to + -ing: the habit of something.

I'm used to waking up early - I'm used to getting up early. I'm getting used to the cold - I'm getting used to it. ⚠️ Not to be confused with used to + infinitive (past habit): I used to smoke vs. I'm used to smoking. After used to (be/get) is always a gerund.

int_u62.note: look forward to / feel like / succeed in
int_u62.exp:
Unit 62 studies fixed verb-prepositional expressions that require a gerund.

Look forward to + -ing: I'm looking forward to seeing you. Succeed in, give up, take up, carry on, go on, put off, keep on - all with a gerund. Feel like + -ing: I don't feel like going out tonight.

int_u63.note: insist on / accuse of / apologise for
int_u63.exp:
Unit 63 continues the theme: verbs + prepositions + gerund.

Frequent expressions: apologize for, accuse of, insist on, prevent from, discourage from, concentrate on, rely on, depend on, thank for, blame for. Example:She apologized for being late. I insist on paying.Be sure to remember the preposition along with the verb.

int_u64.text: Unit 64 - Infinitive to indicate purpose (so that)
int_u64.note: I came to help. / She left so as not to argue.
int_u64.exp:
Unit 64 parses the infinitive and subordinate clauses.

Simple purpose: I called to tell you the news. Negative: so as not to, in order not to: She whispered so as not to wake the baby. Clause of purpose with different subjects: so that + clause: She left early so that I could sleep.

int_u65.note: It's nice to meet you. She's easy to talk to.
int_u65.exp:
Unit 65 explores constructions with infinitive after adjectives.

It's + adj + to-infinitive: It's difficult to understand. It's kind of you to help. Easy/difficult/impossible + to-infinitive: She's easy to talk to. After first, next, last, only: She was the first to arrive.

int_u66.text: Unit 66 – Afraid / interested / sorry: infinitive or gerund
int_u66.note: afraid of doing / afraid to do
int_u66.exp:
Unit 66 looks at adjectives that can be combined with both forms.

Afraid of + -ing: fear of something: She's afraid of flying. Afraid to + infinitive: reluctance due to fear: She was afraid to tell him. Similar: sorry for/about + -ing vs. sorry to do. It is important to understand the difference in meaning.

int_u67.text: Unit 67 — See somebody do or do
int_u67.note: I saw him fall. / I saw him falling.
int_u67.exp:
Unit 67 examines constructions with verbs of perception + object + infinitive/gerund.

Verbs: see, hear, watch, feel, notice, listen to. With an infinitive without to: completed action as a whole: I saw him cross the road. With -ing: in progress: I saw him crossing the road. Similarly: make/let + object + infinitive without to.

int_u68.note: Feeling tired, I went to bed.
int_u68.exp:
Unit 68 explores participial phrases with -ing to concisely express simultaneous or sequential actions.

Leaving the room, she turned off the light (at the same time). Having finished, she went home (after). The subject of the participial phrase must coincide with the subject of the main clause - otherwise there is an error. Style: More bookish/formal.

int_u69.exp:
Unit 69 codifies the difference between countable and uncountable nouns.

Countable: countable, plural, used with a/an. Uncountable: cannot be counted directly, no plural, no article a/an. Typical uncountables: advice, information, news, furniture, luggage, money, work, music, weather. For quantity: a piece of, a bit of.

int_u70.note: hair / a hair / paper / a paper
int_u70.exp:
Unit 70 examines nouns that can be either countable or uncountable with varying meanings.

Hair (hair, uncountable) vs. a hair (one hair). Paper (paper) vs. a paper (newspaper/work). Light, room, time, experience, business and others. Context determines meaning and grammar.

int_u71.text: Unit 71 – Article a/an and the word some
int_u71.note: a book / some books / some milk
int_u71.exp:
Unit 71 explains how some serves as a “plural article.” for countables and “article” for uncountables.

A book / some books is a parallel. Some in indefinite quantities: I need some advice. Any in questions and negations: Do you have any questions? I don't have any money. Exception: sentences with some = Would you like some…?

int_u72.exp:
Unit 72 explains in detail the transition from a/an to the when mentioned again.

First mention: a (unfamiliar to the listener). Repeated: the (already known). I saw a film yesterday. The film was great. The also in front of one-of-a-kind items: the sun, the moon, the president.

int_u73.note: the radio / the only / the same
int_u73.exp:
Unit 73 systematizes the main uses of the definite article the.

The before: single objects (the Earth); already known from the context; with same, only, first, next, last, best: the same problem. In constructions: the more…the more. With adjectives as nouns: the rich, the poor.

int_u74.note: in the morning / on the way / at the same time
int_u74.exp:
Unit 74 analyzes stable combinations with the that you need to remember.

In the morning/afternoon/evening (but at night). On the way. At the same time. In the end (ultimately). By the way. Comparison: in the end (finally) vs. at the end (at the end of something).

int_u75.text: Unit 75 - Article the (lesson 3): general vs. specific
int_u75.note: I like music. / I like the music in this café.
int_u75.exp:
Unit 75 explains the difference between general (without articles) and specific (with the) situations.

General: Music is beautiful. I love coffee. Specific: The music in this café is wonderful. The coffee I had this morning was great. Key rule: if it is clear what exactly we are talking about - the.

int_u76.text: Unit 76 – The article the (lesson 4): with adjectives and nationalities
int_u76.note: the French / the elderly / the rich
int_u76.exp:
Unit 76 explores the with adjectives for groups of people and nationalities.

The + adjective for group: the poor, the elderly, the disabled. Nationalities with the: the French, the Chinese, the British. Without the: French people, a French person. The verb is always plural: The French are known for their cuisine.

int_u77.text: Unit 77 - The article the (lesson 5): with names and place names
int_u77.exp:
Unit 77 systematizes the use of the with proper names and geographical objects.

With the: rivers (the Nile), oceans (the Pacific), mountain ranges (the Alps), countries with Republic/Kingdom/States (the UK). Without the: cities, countries, continents. People's names: without the (Anna Smith), family: the Smiths.

int_u78.text: Unit 78 – Article the (lesson 6): streets, establishments, organizations
int_u78.exp:
Unit 78 examines the use of articles with the names of institutions, streets, establishments.

Without the: streets (Oxford Street), airports (Heathrow Airport), universities without of (Harvard). With the: museums (the British Museum), hotels (the Hilton), organizations (the BBC), universities with of (the University of Oxford).

int_u79.text: Unit 79 - Unit. and many more number of nouns
int_u79.exp:
Unit 79 covers plural formation and nouns with special forms in detail.

Correct: +s/-es/-ies. Incorrect: man→men, woman→women, child→children, foot→feet. Identical forms: sheep, fish, deer. Always plural: police, people, cattle. Always singular: news, mathematics, physics.

int_u80.text: Unit 80 – Two nouns next to each other (word formation)
int_u80.note: a car door / a music teacher / a bus stop
int_u80.exp:
Unit 80explains the noun + noun construction to indicate a type or purpose.

The first noun is a “qualifier”: a car door, a bus stop, a music teacher, a kitchen table. Comparison with of: the door of the car (a specific door of a specific car). The first noun is always singular: a book shop (not books shop).

int_u81.note: Tom's car / the children's room / yesterday's news
int_u81.exp:
Unit 81 parses the possessive case with an apostrophe.

Singular: Tom's, the girl's. Correct plural: the boys' room. Irregular plural: the children's. When is 's better, and when is of: 's - for people and animals. Of - for things: the top of the mountain. Also: yesterday's, today's, this morning's.

int_u82.note: I hurt myself. She enjoyed herself.
int_u82.exp:
Unit 82 studies reflexive pronouns when the subject and object are the same.

Series: myself, yourself, himself, herself, herself, ourselves, yourselves, themselves. To strengthen: I did it myself (personally, without help). Some verbs usually do not require a reflexive: I'm going to wash and get dressed (without myself).

int_u83.note: Is this yours? my own room
int_u83.exp:
Unit 83 looks at possessive pronouns and the construction with own.

Possessive pronouns (without a noun): mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs. Own reinforces the idea of ​​personal belonging: my own room (mine only). On my own = by myself = alone, without help.

int_u84.note: There is a problem. It's raining.
int_u84.exp:
Unit 84 distinguishes between two types of formal subjects.

There - introduces a new subject/person: There is a problem. There are many options. It - for weather, time, distance, general ratings: It's cold. It's Tuesday. It's 10km. You cannot mix: It is a problem in the meaning of existence.

int_u85.note: some milk / any questions / anyone / anything
int_u85.exp:
Unit 85 organizes some/any and their derivatives at the Intermediate level.

Some - in statements and polite sentences. Any - in questions/denials. Any in statements = “any”: You can park anywhere. Derivatives: someone/anyone, something/anything, somewhere/anywhere, nobody/anybody.

int_u86.text: Unit 86 - Nobody, no-one, nothing, nowhere, none
int_u86.exp:
Unit 86 explores negative pronouns with no-.

Nobody/no one, nothing, nowhere, none are used with an affirmative verb: Nobody came. Difference between no + noun (no problem) and none (no noun): How many? - None. After negative pronouns - any, not no: Nobody did anything.

int_u87.note: much water / many people / little time / few friends
int_u87.exp:
Unit 87 examines quantitative pronouns in detail.

Much - uncountable; many - countable plural. Little (little, almost none) vs. a little (a little, enough) - for the uncountable. Few (few, almost no one) vs. a few (several, enough) - for those who are countable. A nuance in the speaker's attitude towards quantity.

int_u88.note: most of us / some of the students / none of them
int_u88.exp:
Unit 88 examines the construction “quantitative word + of + pronoun/definite group”.

With of before pronouns and definite articles: most of us, some of the students, none of them, half of the cake. Without of before indefinite nouns: most people, some students (generalization).

int_u89.note: Both of them / Either road / Neither is correct.
int_u89.exp:
Unit 89 explores pronouns for two objects/people.

Both - both: Both sisters called. Both of them came. Either - any of the two (or): You can take either road. Neither - neither one nor the other: Neither answer is right. Agreement: either/neither in singular. or plural (colloquial). Conjunctions: both…and / either…or / neither…nor.

int_u90.note: all day / the whole day / every day
int_u90.exp:
Unit 90 compares all, whole and every in different contexts.

All vs. whole: all the day = the whole day (all day). Every - each individually: every day, every student. All day (all day) vs. every day (every day). Everybody/everyone - that's it. Everything - everything. Coordination with units verb.

int_u91.exp:
Unit 91 explains the subtle difference between each and every.

Each - individual attention to each member of a group (often small): Each student received a certificate. Every - all together as a group, generalization: Every student passed the exam. Each can come after the subject: They each have a key. Every - only before a noun.

int_u92.note: the man who / the book that / the place where
int_u92.exp:
Unit 92 introduces attributive clauses that qualify the noun.

Basic conjunctions: who/that - for people; which/that - for things; where - for places; when - for time. Place of the subordinate clause: immediately after the noun being defined. If the subordinate clause is an object, the conjunction can be omitted: the book (that) I read.

int_u93.text: Unit 93 – Relative Clauses with which, that, who
int_u93.note: people who / things which / using that
int_u93.exp:
Unit 93 goes into detail about the use of who, which, that.

Who - for people (subject or object). Which - for items. That - for both in restrictive clauses. Whom is the object form of who (formally). When a conjunction is obligatory (subject) and when it is optional (object).

int_u94.text: Unit 94 – Relative Clauses with whose, whom, where
int_u94.note: the man whose car / the woman whom I met
int_u94.exp:
Unit 94 parses less common but important relative words.

Whose - belonging: the man whose car was stolen. Whom is the object form of who (formally): the woman whom I met. Where - for places: the town where I was born. Preposition + whom/which: the company for which she works.

int_u95.note: My sister who lives in Paris / My sister, who lives in Paris
int_u95.exp:
Unit 95 explains the difference between restrictive (without a comma) and descriptive (with a comma) clauses.

Restrictive: specifies who/what exactly we are talking about: The man who called was a doctor. Descriptive: additional information (commas): My father, who is 60, runs marathons. Descriptives are not allowed use that.

int_u96.note: She passed the exam, which surprised everyone.
int_u96.exp:
Unit 96 continues the theme of non-defining clauses with more complex examples.

Which can refer to the entire previous sentence: She got the job, which was great news. Commas are required. You cannot omit who/which. You cannot use that. A subordinate clause often adds a reaction, comment, or addition.

int_u97.note: the woman talking to Tom / the boy injured
int_u97.exp:
Unit 97 parses participial phrases (-ing and -ed) as replacements for relative clauses.

-ing instead of an active clause: the man sitting by the window = the man who is sitting. -ed instead of passive: the car stolen last week = the car that was stolen. A compressed form characteristic of written speech.

int_u98.exp:
Unit 98explains pairs of adjectives formed from verbs: causing sensation vs. the experience of a sensation.

-ing - that which causes the sensation: The film is boring (the film is boring). -ed - someone who experiences the feeling: I'm bored (I'm bored). Error: I'm very boring → I'm very bored. Pairs: exciting/excited, tiring/tired, interesting/interested.

int_u99.note: a beautiful big old Italian stone house
int_u99.exp:
Unit 99 breaks down the order of several adjectives before a noun.

Order: opinion → size → age → shape → color → origin → material. Example: a lovely small old square black English oak table. In practice, more than 2-3 adjectives are rarely used. Key: opinion/subjective comes first.
